{{infobox Shinto shrine |name= Ōmizu Shrine, Kawaai Shrine, Kumabuchi Shrine |image= [[File:Ōmizu-jinja 01.jpg|270px]] |location= {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, Uji Imazai-kechō, Hayasakiguchi 163-1, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} |latitude_deg = 34 |latitude_min = 27 |latitude_sec = 33.8 |longitude_deg = 136 |longitude_min = 43 |longitude_sec = 17.5 |map= Japan Mie |enshrined_deity= Ōmizu Shrine: Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto
Kawaai Shrine: Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami
Kumabuchi Shrine: Takiootogi-no-Kami |status= Ōmizu Shrine: {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} auxiliary shrine
Kawaai Shrine & Kumabuchi Shrine: Kōtai Jingū subordinate shrines |established= Traditionally attributed to the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}} |main_hall_style= [[Shinmei-zukuri]] |alternate_names= |sacred_sites= |annual_festival= |rituals= {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} }} '''Ōmizu Shrine''' (大水神社) is an auxiliary shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|zh|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}. This article also covers the subordinate shrines '''Kawaai Shrine''' (川相神社) and '''Kumabuchi Shrine''' (熊淵神社), which are co-located with Ōmizu Shrine. All three shrines are said to have been established by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}. Ōmizu Shrine enshrines a {{ill|Mountain God|ja|山の神|en}}, while Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine enshrine {{ill|water deities|ja|水神}}.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 == Overview == These three shrines are located at Uji Imazai-kechō, Hayasakiguchi 163-1, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, about 200 meters from the {{ill|Uji Bridge (Ise)|ja|宇治橋}} at the entrance to the Inner Shrine's sacred precinct.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 111 About 50 meters ahead is {{ill|Aedo Hashihime Shrine|ja|饗土橋姫神社}}, and 70 meters ahead are the auxiliary shrines {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社}}, {{ill|Shinkawa Shrine|ja|新川神社}}, and {{ill|Ishii Shrine|ja|石井神社}}, making a cluster of seven shrines.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): pp. 111, 116 The former {{ill|Hayasakibunko|ja|林崎文庫}}, predecessor of the {{ill|Jingū Library|ja|神宮文庫}}, is also located here.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 117 After ascending 12 stone steps, there is a {{ill|shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居}} and a single shrine building where all three shrines are co-located. An ancient camphor tree with a trunk diameter over 2 meters covers the shrine.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 The shrine, built in [[shinmei-zukuri]] style with plank roofing, faces south and is surrounded by a single {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣}} (sacred fence).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 245 The shrine measures approximately 1.8 m wide, 1.2 m deep, and 2.4 m high. No {{ill|saisenbako|ja|賽銭箱}} (offering box) is present. The shrine grounds cover 228 m² (approximately 69 tsubo).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 244 According to the ''Enryaku Gishikichō,'' the ancient precinct covered about 24,793 m² but has been reduced due to encroachments from private houses, temples, and the Hayasakibunko. Parts of the grounds on the north and east sides are lined with {{ill|stone walls|ja|石垣}}. == Ōmizu Shrine == Ōmizu Shrine is ranked 16th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–10 The enshrined deity is Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto (大山祇御祖命), sometimes written as "大山'''罪乃'''御祖命" in old texts.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 10Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 243 This deity is considered a child of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾命}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊弉冉命}}, worshipped as a mountain deity. Despite its name, meaning "great water shrine," the enshrined deity is a mountain god, which has puzzled some scholars. == Kawaai Shrine == Kawaai Shrine is ranked 10th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 13–14 The enshrined deity is Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami (細川水神), literally "narrow river water deity," a river god. == Kumabuchi Shrine == Kumabuchi Shrine is ranked 11th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine. The enshrined deity is Takiootogi-no-Kami (多支大刀自神), a guardian deity of sacred springs, considered a child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命). == History == According to the ''Jingū Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines Register'' issued in 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine was founded during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' records that a {{ill|shukube|ja|祝部}} was appointed to the shrine, with reconstruction handled by local officials. A record from 1482 confirms that the shrine still existed at that time. After falling into disrepair in the medieval period, the shrine was revived in 1664 under the leadership of {{ill|Kawabe no Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}}. In 1871, Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine were moved to share the same grounds as Ōmizu Shrine. These two shrines were originally located upstream along the {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}. == Rituals == The rituals at Ōmizu Shrine follow the same schedule as those at {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社}}. Ceremonies are held during the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} (June & December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} (October), and {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭}} (November).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 250 Other rituals, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭}} (January 3), National Foundation Day Festival (February 11), {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭}} (February 23), and {{ill|Kaze-no-hi-no-matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}} (May & August), are conducted remotely. == Flora == A 1977 survey of the shrine grounds identified 30 trees, including camphor, cedar, fig, chinquapin, and Japanese yew. == Access == ; To Uji Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} (JR) or {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}}. Get off at the "Naikū-mae" stop.Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Grand Shrine (Inner Shrine) Information]" (Accessed October 13, 2013) * Accessible from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} via Mie Kotsu bus. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} or {{ill|National Route 23|ja|国道23号}} from {{ill|Ise IC|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}}. Parking is available nearby. ; From Uji Bridge * About 5 minutes (240 m) on foot from Uji Bridge rotary and Naikū-mae parking lot. ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Pilgrimage'' (Supplement to ''Isejin''), Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (ed.) ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1'', Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * {{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング}} ''Let's Go to Ise Jingu'', Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * {{ill|Shikinaisha Research Association|ja|式内社研究会}} (ed.) ''Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 6 Tōkaidō 1'', Kōgakkan University Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pages. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m01/0103.html Ōmizu Shrine (大水神社) - Co-located with Kawai Shrine and Kumafuchi Shrine] - Japan Ise Jingu Devotees Association Foundation [[Category:Shrines in Ise]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise Jingu]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines]] [[ja:大水神社]] {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ōmizu Shrine-qq]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ōmizu Shrine-qq]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ōmizu Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:大水神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]] [[ja:Ōmizu Shrine]]

''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1'', Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * {{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング}} ''Let's Go to Ise Jingu'', Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * {{ill|Shikinaisha Research Association|ja|式内社研究会}} (ed.) ''Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 6 Tōkaidō 1'', Kōgakkan University Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pages. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m01/0103.html Ōmizu Shrine (大水神社) - Co-located with Kawai Shrine and Kumafuchi Shrine] - Japan Ise Jingu Devotees Association Foundation [[Category:Shrines in Ise]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise Jingu]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines]] [[ja:大水神社]] {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:Ugoodbye $1]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ōmizu Shrine-qq]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Ugoodbye $1]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ōmizu Shrine-qq]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ōmizu Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:大水神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]] [[ja:Ōmizu Shrine]]

{{infobox Shinto shrine |name= Ōmizu Shrine, Kawaai Shrine, Kumabuchi Shrine |image= [[File:Ōmizu-jinja 01.jpg|270px]] |location= {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, Uji Imazai-kechō, Hayasakiguchi 163-1, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} |latitude_deg = 34 |latitude_min = 27 |latitude_sec = 33.8 |longitude_deg = 136 |longitude_min = 43 |longitude_sec = 17.5 |map= Japan Mie |enshrined_deity= Ōmizu Shrine: Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto
Kawaai Shrine: Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami
Kumabuchi Shrine: Takiootogi-no-Kami |status= Ōmizu Shrine: {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} auxiliary shrine
Kawaai Shrine & Kumabuchi Shrine: Kōtai Jingū subordinate shrines |established= Traditionally attributed to the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}} |main_hall_style= [[Shinmei-zukuri]] |alternate_names= |sacred_sites= |annual_festival= |rituals= {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} }} '''Ōmizu Shrine''' (大水神社) is an auxiliary shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|zh|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}. This article also covers the subordinate shrines '''Kawaai Shrine''' (川相神社) and '''Kumabuchi Shrine''' (熊淵神社), which are co-located with Ōmizu Shrine. All three shrines are said to have been established by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}. Ōmizu Shrine enshrines a {{ill|Mountain God|ja|山の神|en}}, while Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine enshrine {{ill|water deities|ja|水神}}.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 == Overview == These three shrines are located at Uji Imazai-kechō, Hayasakiguchi 163-1, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, about 200 meters from the {{ill|Uji Bridge (Ise)|ja|宇治橋}} at the entrance to the Inner Shrine's sacred precinct.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 111 About 50 meters ahead is {{ill|Aedo Hashihime Shrine|ja|饗土橋姫神社}}, and 70 meters ahead are the auxiliary shrines {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社}}, {{ill|Shinkawa Shrine|ja|新川神社}}, and {{ill|Ishii Shrine|ja|石井神社}}, making a cluster of seven shrines.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): pp. 111, 116 The former {{ill|Hayasakibunko|ja|林崎文庫}}, predecessor of the {{ill|Jingū Library|ja|神宮文庫}}, is also located here.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 117 After ascending 12 stone steps, there is a {{ill|shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居}} and a single shrine building where all three shrines are co-located. An ancient camphor tree with a trunk diameter over 2 meters covers the shrine.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 The shrine, built in [[shinmei-zukuri]] style with plank roofing, faces south and is surrounded by a single {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣}} (sacred fence).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 245 The shrine measures approximately 1.8 m wide, 1.2 m deep, and 2.4 m high. No {{ill|saisenbako|ja|賽銭箱}} (offering box) is present. The shrine grounds cover 228 m² (approximately 69 tsubo).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 244 According to the ''Enryaku Gishikichō,'' the ancient precinct covered about 24,793 m² but has been reduced due to encroachments from private houses, temples, and the Hayasakibunko. Parts of the grounds on the north and east sides are lined with {{ill|stone walls|ja|石垣}}. == Ōmizu Shrine == Ōmizu Shrine is ranked 16th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–10 The enshrined deity is Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto (大山祇御祖命), sometimes written as "大山'''罪乃'''御祖命" in old texts.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 10Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 243 This deity is considered a child of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾命}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊弉冉命}}, worshipped as a mountain deity. Despite its name, meaning "great water shrine," the enshrined deity is a mountain god, which has puzzled some scholars. == Kawaai Shrine == Kawaai Shrine is ranked 10th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 13–14 The enshrined deity is Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami (細川水神), literally "narrow river water deity," a river god. == Kumabuchi Shrine == Kumabuchi Shrine is ranked 11th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine. The enshrined deity is Takiootogi-no-Kami (多支大刀自神), a guardian deity of sacred springs, considered a child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命). == History == According to the ''Jingū Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines Register'' issued in 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine was founded during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' records that a {{ill|shukube|ja|祝部}} was appointed to the shrine, with reconstruction handled by local officials. A record from 1482 confirms that the shrine still existed at that time. After falling into disrepair in the medieval period, the shrine was revived in 1664 under the leadership of {{ill|Kawabe no Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}}. In 1871, Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine were moved to share the same grounds as Ōmizu Shrine. These two shrines were originally located upstream along the {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}. == Rituals == The rituals at Ōmizu Shrine follow the same schedule as those at {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社}}. Ceremonies are held during the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} (June & December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} (October), and {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭}} (November).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 250 Other rituals, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭}} (January 3), National Foundation Day Festival (February 11), {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭}} (February 23), and {{ill|Kaze-no-hi-no-matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}} (May & August), are conducted remotely. == Flora == A 1977 survey of the shrine grounds identified 30 trees, including camphor, cedar, fig, chinquapin, and Japanese yew. == Access == ; To Uji Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} (JR) or {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}}. Get off at the "Naikū-mae" stop.Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Grand Shrine (Inner Shrine) Information]" (Accessed October 13, 2013) * Accessible from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} via Mie Kotsu bus. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} or {{ill|National Route 23|ja|国道23号}} from {{ill|Ise IC|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}}. Parking is available nearby. ; From Uji Bridge * About 5 minutes (240 m) on foot from Uji Bridge rotary and Naikū-mae parking lot. [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Water deities in Shinto]] [[ja:大水神社]] == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise Jingu|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧}} == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Ise Bunka-sha (ed.) ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Pilgrimage'' (Supplement to ''Isejin''), Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (ed.) ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1'', Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * {{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング}} ''Let's Go to Ise Jingu'', Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * {{ill|Shikinaisha Research Association|ja|式内社研究会}} (ed.) ''Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 6 Tōkaidō 1'', Kōgakkan University Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pages. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m01/0103.html Ōmizu Shrine (大水神社) - Co-located with Kawai Shrine and Kumafuchi Shrine] - Japan Ise Jingu Devotees Association Foundation [[Category:Shrines in Ise]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise Jingu]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines]] [[ja:大水神社]] {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Categoery:qq]] [[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:Ugoodbye $1]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ōmizu Shrine-qq]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Ugoodbye $1]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ōmizu Shrine-qq]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ōmizu Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:大水神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]] [[ja:Ōmizu Shrine]]

Kawaai Shrine: Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami
Kumabuchi Shrine: Takiootogi-no-Kami |status= Ōmizu Shrine: {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} auxiliary shrine
Kawaai Shrine & Kumabuchi Shrine: Kōtai Jingū subordinate shrines |established= Traditionally attributed to the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}} |main_hall_style= [[Shinmei-zukuri]] |alternate_names= |sacred_sites= |annual_festival= |rituals= {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} }} '''Ōmizu Shrine''' (大水神社) is an auxiliary shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|zh|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}. This article also covers the subordinate shrines '''Kawaai Shrine''' (川相神社) and '''Kumabuchi Shrine''' (熊淵神社), which are co-located with Ōmizu Shrine. All three shrines are said to have been established by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}. Ōmizu Shrine enshrines a {{ill|Mountain God|ja|山の神|en}}, while Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine enshrine {{ill|water deities|ja|水神}}.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 == Overview == These three shrines are located at Uji Imazai-kechō, Hayasakiguchi 163-1, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, about 200 meters from the {{ill|Uji Bridge (Ise)|ja|宇治橋}} at the entrance to the Inner Shrine's sacred precinct.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 111 About 50 meters ahead is {{ill|Aedo Hashihime Shrine|ja|饗土橋姫神社}}, and 70 meters ahead are the auxiliary shrines {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社}}, {{ill|Shinkawa Shrine|ja|新川神社}}, and {{ill|Ishii Shrine|ja|石井神社}}, making a cluster of seven shrines.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): pp. 111, 116 The former {{ill|Hayasakibunko|ja|林崎文庫}}, predecessor of the {{ill|Jingū Library|ja|神宮文庫}}, is also located here.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 117 After ascending 12 stone steps, there is a {{ill|shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居}} and a single shrine building where all three shrines are co-located. An ancient camphor tree with a trunk diameter over 2 meters covers the shrine.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 The shrine, built in [[shinmei-zukuri]] style with plank roofing, faces south and is surrounded by a single {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣}} (sacred fence).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 245 The shrine measures approximately 1.8 m wide, 1.2 m deep, and 2.4 m high. No {{ill|saisenbako|ja|賽銭箱}} (offering box) is present. The shrine grounds cover 228 m² (approximately 69 tsubo).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 244 According to the ''Enryaku Gishikichō,'' the ancient precinct covered about 24,793 m² but has been reduced due to encroachments from private houses, temples, and the Hayasakibunko. Parts of the grounds on the north and east sides are lined with {{ill|stone walls|ja|石垣}}. == Ōmizu Shrine == Ōmizu Shrine is ranked 16th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–10 The enshrined deity is Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto (大山祇御祖命), sometimes written as "大山'''罪乃'''御祖命" in old texts.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 10Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 243 This deity is considered a child of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾命}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊弉冉命}}, worshipped as a mountain deity. Despite its name, meaning "great water shrine," the enshrined deity is a mountain god, which has puzzled some scholars. == Kawaai Shrine == Kawaai Shrine is ranked 10th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 13–14 The enshrined deity is Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami (細川水神), literally "narrow river water deity," a river god. == Kumabuchi Shrine == Kumabuchi Shrine is ranked 11th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine. The enshrined deity is Takiootogi-no-Kami (多支大刀自神), a guardian deity of sacred springs, considered a child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命). == History == According to the ''Jingū Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines Register'' issued in 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine was founded during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' records that a {{ill|shukube|ja|祝部}} was appointed to the shrine, with reconstruction handled by local officials. A record from 1482 confirms that the shrine still existed at that time. After falling into disrepair in the medieval period, the shrine was revived in 1664 under the leadership of {{ill|Kawabe no Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}}. In 1871, Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine were moved to share the same grounds as Ōmizu Shrine. These two shrines were originally located upstream along the {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}. == Rituals == The rituals at Ōmizu Shrine follow the same schedule as those at {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社}}. Ceremonies are held during the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} (June & December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} (October), and {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭}} (November).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 250 Other rituals, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭}} (January 3), National Foundation Day Festival (February 11), {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭}} (February 23), and {{ill|Kaze-no-hi-no-matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}} (May & August), are conducted remotely. == Flora == A 1977 survey of the shrine grounds identified 30 trees, including camphor, cedar, fig, chinquapin, and Japanese yew. == Access == ; To Uji Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} (JR) or {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}}. Get off at the "Naikū-mae" stop.Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Grand Shrine (Inner Shrine) Information]" (Accessed October 13, 2013) * Accessible from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} via Mie Kotsu bus. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} or {{ill|National Route 23|ja|国道23号}} from {{ill|Ise IC|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}}. Parking is available nearby. ; From Uji Bridge * About 5 minutes (240 m) on foot from Uji Bridge rotary and Naikū-mae parking lot. [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Water deities in Shinto]] [[ja:大水神社]] == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise Jingu|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧}} == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Ise Bunka-sha (ed.) ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Pilgrimage'' (Supplement to ''Isejin''), Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (ed.) ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1'', Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * {{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング}} ''Let's Go to Ise Jingu'', Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * {{ill|Shikinaisha Research Association|ja|式内社研究会}} (ed.) ''Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 6 Tōkaidō 1'', Kōgakkan University Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pages. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m01/0103.html Ōmizu Shrine (大水神社) - Co-located with Kawai Shrine and Kumafuchi Shrine] - Japan Ise Jingu Devotees Association Foundation [[Category:Shrines in Ise]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise Jingu]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines]] [[ja:大水神社]] {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category: :qq]] [[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:Ugoodbye $1]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ōmizu Shrine-qq]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Ugoodbye $1]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ōmizu Shrine-qq]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ōmizu Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:大水神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]] [[ja:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[Category:New]]

No {{ill|saisenbako|ja|賽銭箱}} (offering box) is present. The shrine grounds cover 228 m² (approximately 69 tsubo).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 244 According to the ''Enryaku Gishikichō,'' the ancient precinct covered about 24,793 m² but has been reduced due to encroachments from private houses, temples, and the Hayasakibunko. Parts of the grounds on the north and east sides are lined with {{ill|stone walls|ja|石垣}}. == Ōmizu Shrine == Ōmizu Shrine is ranked 16th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–10 The enshrined deity is Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto (大山祇御祖命), sometimes written as "大山'''罪乃'''御祖命" in old texts.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 10Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 243 This deity is considered a child of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾命}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊弉冉命}}, worshipped as a mountain deity. Despite its name, meaning "great water shrine," the enshrined deity is a mountain god, which has puzzled some scholars. == Kawaai Shrine == Kawaai Shrine is ranked 10th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 13–14 The enshrined deity is Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami (細川水神), literally "narrow river water deity," a river god. == Kumabuchi Shrine == Kumabuchi Shrine is ranked 11th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine. The enshrined deity is Takiootogi-no-Kami (多支大刀自神), a guardian deity of sacred springs, considered a child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命). == History == According to the ''Jingū Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines Register'' issued in 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine was founded during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' records that a {{ill|shukube|ja|祝部}} was appointed to the shrine, with reconstruction handled by local officials. A record from 1482 confirms that the shrine still existed at that time. After falling into disrepair in the medieval period, the shrine was revived in 1664 under the leadership of {{ill|Kawabe no Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}}. In 1871, Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine were moved to share the same grounds as Ōmizu Shrine. These two shrines were originally located upstream along the {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}. == Rituals == The rituals at Ōmizu Shrine follow the same schedule as those at {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社}}. Ceremonies are held during the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} (June & December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} (October), and {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭}} (November).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 250 Other rituals, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭}} (January 3), National Foundation Day Festival (February 11), {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭}} (February 23), and {{ill|Kaze-no-hi-no-matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}} (May & August), are conducted remotely. == Flora == A 1977 survey of the shrine grounds identified 30 trees, including camphor, cedar, fig, chinquapin, and Japanese yew. == Access == ; To Uji Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} (JR) or {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}}. Get off at the "Naikū-mae" stop.Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Grand Shrine (Inner Shrine) Information]" (Accessed October 13, 2013) * Accessible from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} via Mie Kotsu bus. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} or {{ill|National Route 23|ja|国道23号}} from {{ill|Ise IC|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}}. Parking is available nearby. ; From Uji Bridge * About 5 minutes (240 m) on foot from Uji Bridge rotary and Naikū-mae parking lot. [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Water deities in Shinto]] [[ja:大水神社]] == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise Jingu|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧}} == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Ise Bunka-sha (ed.) ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Pilgrimage'' (Supplement to ''Isejin''), Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (ed.) ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1'', Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * {{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング}} ''Let's Go to Ise Jingu'', Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * {{ill|Shikinaisha Research Association|ja|式内社研究会}} (ed.) ''Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 6 Tōkaidō 1'', Kōgakkan University Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pages. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m01/0103.html Ōmizu Shrine (大水神社) - Co-located with Kawai Shrine and Kumafuchi Shrine] - Japan Ise Jingu Devotees Association Foundation [[Category:Shrines in Ise]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise Jingu]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines]] [[ja:大水神社]] {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category: :qq]] [[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ōmizu Shrine-qq]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ōmizu Shrine-qq]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ōmizu Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:大水神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]] [[ja:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[Category:New]]

{{infobox Shinto shrine |name= Ōmizu Shrine, Kawaai Shrine, Kumabuchi Shrine |image= [[File:Ōmizu-jinja 01.jpg|270px]] |location= {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, Uji Imazai-kechō, Hayasakiguchi 163-1, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} |latitude_deg = 34 |latitude_min = 27 |latitude_sec = 33.8 |longitude_deg = 136 |longitude_min = 43 |longitude_sec = 17.5 |map= Japan Mie |enshrined_deity= Ōmizu Shrine: Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto
Kawaai Shrine: Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami
Kumabuchi Shrine: Takiootogi-no-Kami |status= Ōmizu Shrine: {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} auxiliary shrine
Kawaai Shrine & Kumabuchi Shrine: Kōtai Jingū subordinate shrines |established= Traditionally attributed to the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}} |main_hall_style= [[Shinmei-zukuri]] |alternate_names= |sacred_sites= |annual_festival= |rituals= {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} }} '''Ōmizu Shrine''' (大水神社) is an auxiliary shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|zh|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}. This article also covers the subordinate shrines '''Kawaai Shrine''' (川相神社) and '''Kumabuchi Shrine''' (熊淵神社), which are co-located with Ōmizu Shrine. All three shrines are said to have been established by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}. Ōmizu Shrine enshrines a {{ill|Mountain God|ja|山の神|en}}, while Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine enshrine {{ill|water deities|ja|水神}}.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 == Overview == These three shrines are located at Uji Imazai-kechō, Hayasakiguchi 163-1, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, about 200 meters from the {{ill|Uji Bridge (Ise)|ja|宇治橋}} at the entrance to the Inner Shrine's sacred precinct.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 111 About 50 meters ahead is {{ill|Aedo Hashihime Shrine|ja|饗土橋姫神社}}, and 70 meters ahead are the auxiliary shrines {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社}}, {{ill|Shinkawa Shrine|ja|新川神社}}, and {{ill|Ishii Shrine|ja|石井神社}}, making a cluster of seven shrines.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): pp. 111, 116 The former {{ill|Hayasakibunko|ja|林崎文庫}}, predecessor of the {{ill|Jingū Library|ja|神宮文庫}}, is also located here.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 117 After ascending 12 stone steps, there is a {{ill|shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居}} and a single shrine building where all three shrines are co-located. An ancient camphor tree with a trunk diameter over 2 meters covers the shrine.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 The shrine, built in [[shinmei-zukuri]] style with plank roofing, faces south and is surrounded by a single {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣}} (sacred fence).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 245 The shrine measures approximately 1.8 m wide, 1.2 m deep, and 2.4 m high. No {{ill|saisenbako|ja|賽銭箱}} (offering box) is present. The shrine grounds cover 228 m² (approximately 69 tsubo).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 244 According to the ''Enryaku Gishikichō,'' the ancient precinct covered about 24,793 m² but has been reduced due to encroachments from private houses, temples, and the Hayasakibunko. Parts of the grounds on the north and east sides are lined with {{ill|stone walls|ja|石垣}}. == Ōmizu Shrine == Ōmizu Shrine is ranked 16th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–10 The enshrined deity is Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto (大山祇御祖命), sometimes written as "大山'''罪乃'''御祖命" in old texts.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 10Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 243 This deity is considered a child of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾命}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊弉冉命}}, worshipped as a mountain deity. Despite its name, meaning "great water shrine," the enshrined deity is a mountain god, which has puzzled some scholars. == Kawaai Shrine == Kawaai Shrine is ranked 10th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 13–14 The enshrined deity is Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami (細川水神), literally "narrow river water deity," a river god. == Kumabuchi Shrine == Kumabuchi Shrine is ranked 11th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine. The enshrined deity is Takiootogi-no-Kami (多支大刀自神), a guardian deity of sacred springs, considered a child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命). == History == According to the ''Jingū Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines Register'' issued in 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine was founded during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' records that a {{ill|shukube|ja|祝部}} was appointed to the shrine, with reconstruction handled by local officials. A record from 1482 confirms that the shrine still existed at that time. After falling into disrepair in the medieval period, the shrine was revived in 1664 under the leadership of {{ill|Kawabe no Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}}. In 1871, Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine were moved to share the same grounds as Ōmizu Shrine. These two shrines were originally located upstream along the {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}. == Rituals == The rituals at Ōmizu Shrine follow the same schedule as those at {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社}}. Ceremonies are held during the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} (June & December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} (October), and {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭}} (November).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 250 Other rituals, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭}} (January 3), National Foundation Day Festival (February 11), {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭}} (February 23), and {{ill|Kaze-no-hi-no-matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}} (May & August), are conducted remotely. == Flora == A 1977 survey of the shrine grounds identified 30 trees, including camphor, cedar, fig, chinquapin, and Japanese yew. == Access == ; To Uji Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} (JR) or {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}}. Get off at the "Naikū-mae" stop.Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Grand Shrine (Inner Shrine) Information]" (Accessed October 13, 2013) * Accessible from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} via Mie Kotsu bus. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} or {{ill|National Route 23|ja|国道23号}} from {{ill|Ise IC|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}}. Parking is available nearby. ; From Uji Bridge * About 5 minutes (240 m) on foot from Uji Bridge rotary and Naikū-mae parking lot. [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Water deities in Shinto]] [[ja:大水神社]] == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise Jingu|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧}} == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Ise Bunka-sha (ed.) ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Pilgrimage'' (Supplement to ''Isejin''), Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (ed.) ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1'', Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * {{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング}} ''Let's Go to Ise Jingu'', Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * {{ill|Shikinaisha Research Association|ja|式内社研究会}} (ed.) ''Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 6 Tōkaidō 1'', Kōgakkan University Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pages. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m01/0103.html Ōmizu Shrine (大水神社) - Co-located with Kawai Shrine and Kumafuchi Shrine] - Japan Ise Jingu Devotees Association Foundation [[Category:Shrines in Ise]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise Jingu]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines]] [[ja:大水神社]] {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ōmizu Shrine-qq]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ōmizu Shrine-qq]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ōmizu Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:大水神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]] [[ja:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[Category:New]]

These two shrines were originally located upstream along the {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}. == Rituals == The rituals at Ōmizu Shrine follow the same schedule as those at {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社}}. Ceremonies are held during the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} (June & December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} (October), and {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭}} (November).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 250 Other rituals, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭}} (January 3), National Foundation Day Festival (February 11), {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭}} (February 23), and {{ill|Kaze-no-hi-no-matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}} (May & August), are conducted remotely. == Flora == A 1977 survey of the shrine grounds identified 30 trees, including camphor, cedar, fig, chinquapin, and Japanese yew. == Access == ; To Uji Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} (JR) or {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}}. Get off at the "Naikū-mae" stop.Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Grand Shrine (Inner Shrine) Information]" (Accessed October 13, 2013) * Accessible from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} via Mie Kotsu bus. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} or {{ill|National Route 23|ja|国道23号}} from {{ill|Ise IC|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}}. Parking is available nearby. ; From Uji Bridge * About 5 minutes (240 m) on foot from Uji Bridge rotary and Naikū-mae parking lot. [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Water deities in Shinto]] [[ja:大水神社]] == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise Jingu|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧}} == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Ise Bunka-sha (ed.) ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Pilgrimage'' (Supplement to ''Isejin''), Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (ed.) ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1'', Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * {{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング}} ''Let's Go to Ise Jingu'', Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * {{ill|Shikinaisha Research Association|ja|式内社研究会}} (ed.) ''Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 6 Tōkaidō 1'', Kōgakkan University Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pages. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m01/0103.html Ōmizu Shrine (大水神社) - Co-located with Kawai Shrine and Kumafuchi Shrine] - Japan Ise Jingu Devotees Association Foundation [[Category:Shrines in Ise]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise Jingu]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines]] [[ja:大水神社]] {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:qq-Ōmizu Shrine-qq]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:qq-Ōmizu Shrine-qq]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ōmizu Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:大水神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]] [[ja:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[Category:New]]

{{infobox Shinto shrine |name= Ōmizu Shrine, Kawaai Shrine, Kumabuchi Shrine |image= [[File:Ōmizu-jinja 01.jpg|270px]] |location= {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, Uji Imazai-kechō, Hayasakiguchi 163-1, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} |latitude_deg = 34 |latitude_min = 27 |latitude_sec = 33.8 |longitude_deg = 136 |longitude_min = 43 |longitude_sec = 17.5 |map= Japan Mie |enshrined_deity= Ōmizu Shrine: Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto
Kawaai Shrine: Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami
Kumabuchi Shrine: Takiootogi-no-Kami |status= Ōmizu Shrine: {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} auxiliary shrine
Kawaai Shrine & Kumabuchi Shrine: Kōtai Jingū subordinate shrines |established= Traditionally attributed to the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}} |main_hall_style= [[Shinmei-zukuri]] |alternate_names= |sacred_sites= |annual_festival= |rituals= {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} }} '''Ōmizu Shrine''' (大水神社) is an auxiliary shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|zh|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}. This article also covers the subordinate shrines '''Kawaai Shrine''' (川相神社) and '''Kumabuchi Shrine''' (熊淵神社), which are co-located with Ōmizu Shrine. All three shrines are said to have been established by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}. Ōmizu Shrine enshrines a {{ill|Mountain God|ja|山の神|en}}, while Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine enshrine {{ill|water deities|ja|水神}}.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 == Overview == These three shrines are located at Uji Imazai-kechō, Hayasakiguchi 163-1, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, about 200 meters from the {{ill|Uji Bridge (Ise)|ja|宇治橋}} at the entrance to the Inner Shrine's sacred precinct.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 111 About 50 meters ahead is {{ill|Aedo Hashihime Shrine|ja|饗土橋姫神社}}, and 70 meters ahead are the auxiliary shrines {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社}}, {{ill|Shinkawa Shrine|ja|新川神社}}, and {{ill|Ishii Shrine|ja|石井神社}}, making a cluster of seven shrines.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): pp. 111, 116 The former {{ill|Hayasakibunko|ja|林崎文庫}}, predecessor of the {{ill|Jingū Library|ja|神宮文庫}}, is also located here.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 117 After ascending 12 stone steps, there is a {{ill|shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居}} and a single shrine building where all three shrines are co-located. An ancient camphor tree with a trunk diameter over 2 meters covers the shrine.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 The shrine, built in [[shinmei-zukuri]] style with plank roofing, faces south and is surrounded by a single {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣}} (sacred fence).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 245 The shrine measures approximately 1.8 m wide, 1.2 m deep, and 2.4 m high. No {{ill|saisenbako|ja|賽銭箱}} (offering box) is present. The shrine grounds cover 228 m² (approximately 69 tsubo).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 244 According to the ''Enryaku Gishikichō,'' the ancient precinct covered about 24,793 m² but has been reduced due to encroachments from private houses, temples, and the Hayasakibunko. Parts of the grounds on the north and east sides are lined with {{ill|stone walls|ja|石垣}}. == Ōmizu Shrine == Ōmizu Shrine is ranked 16th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–10 The enshrined deity is Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto (大山祇御祖命), sometimes written as "大山'''罪乃'''御祖命" in old texts.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 10Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 243 This deity is considered a child of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾命}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊弉冉命}}, worshipped as a mountain deity. Despite its name, meaning "great water shrine," the enshrined deity is a mountain god, which has puzzled some scholars. == Kawaai Shrine == Kawaai Shrine is ranked 10th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 13–14 The enshrined deity is Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami (細川水神), literally "narrow river water deity," a river god. == Kumabuchi Shrine == Kumabuchi Shrine is ranked 11th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine. The enshrined deity is Takiootogi-no-Kami (多支大刀自神), a guardian deity of sacred springs, considered a child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命). == History == According to the ''Jingū Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines Register'' issued in 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine was founded during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' records that a {{ill|shukube|ja|祝部}} was appointed to the shrine, with reconstruction handled by local officials. A record from 1482 confirms that the shrine still existed at that time. After falling into disrepair in the medieval period, the shrine was revived in 1664 under the leadership of {{ill|Kawabe no Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}}. In 1871, Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine were moved to share the same grounds as Ōmizu Shrine. These two shrines were originally located upstream along the {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}. == Rituals == The rituals at Ōmizu Shrine follow the same schedule as those at {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社}}. Ceremonies are held during the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} (June & December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} (October), and {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭}} (November).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 250 Other rituals, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭}} (January 3), National Foundation Day Festival (February 11), {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭}} (February 23), and {{ill|Kaze-no-hi-no-matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}} (May & August), are conducted remotely. == Flora == A 1977 survey of the shrine grounds identified 30 trees, including camphor, cedar, fig, chinquapin, and Japanese yew. == Access == ; To Uji Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} (JR) or {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}}. Get off at the "Naikū-mae" stop.Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Grand Shrine (Inner Shrine) Information]" (Accessed October 13, 2013) * Accessible from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} via Mie Kotsu bus. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} or {{ill|National Route 23|ja|国道23号}} from {{ill|Ise IC|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}}. Parking is available nearby. ; From Uji Bridge * About 5 minutes (240 m) on foot from Uji Bridge rotary and Naikū-mae parking lot. [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Water deities in Shinto]] [[ja:大水神社]] == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise Jingu|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧}} == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Ise Bunka-sha (ed.) ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Pilgrimage'' (Supplement to ''Isejin''), Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (ed.) ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1'', Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * {{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング}} ''Let's Go to Ise Jingu'', Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * {{ill|Shikinaisha Research Association|ja|式内社研究会}} (ed.) ''Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 6 Tōkaidō 1'', Kōgakkan University Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pages. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m01/0103.html Ōmizu Shrine (大水神社) - Co-located with Kawai Shrine and Kumafuchi Shrine] - Japan Ise Jingu Devotees Association Foundation [[Category:Shrines in Ise]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise Jingu]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines]] [[ja:大水神社]] {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:qq-Ōmizu Shrine—>[[category:qq close]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:qq-Ōmizu Shrine—>[[category:qq close]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ōmizu Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:大水神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]] [[ja:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[Category:New]]

''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Pilgrimage'' (Supplement to ''Isejin''), Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (ed.) ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1'', Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * {{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング}} ''Let's Go to Ise Jingu'', Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * {{ill|Shikinaisha Research Association|ja|式内社研究会}} (ed.) ''Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 6 Tōkaidō 1'', Kōgakkan University Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pages. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m01/0103.html Ōmizu Shrine (大水神社) - Co-located with Kawai Shrine and Kumafuchi Shrine] - Japan Ise Jingu Devotees Association Foundation [[Category:Shrines in Ise]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise Jingu]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines]] [[ja:大水神社]] {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:deprecated qq open]]

revision.text:
{{infobox Shinto shrine |name= Ōmizu Shrine, Kawaai Shrine, Kumabuchi Shrine |image= [[File:Ōmizu-jinja 01.jpg|270px]] |location= {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, Uji Imazai-kechō, Hayasakiguchi 163-1, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} |latitude_deg = 34 |latitude_min = 27 |latitude_sec = 33.8 |longitude_deg = 136 |longitude_min = 43 |longitude_sec = 17.5 |map= Japan Mie |enshrined_deity= Ōmizu Shrine: Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto
Kawaai Shrine: Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami
Kumabuchi Shrine: Takiootogi-no-Kami |status= Ōmizu Shrine: {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} auxiliary shrine
Kawaai Shrine & Kumabuchi Shrine: Kōtai Jingū subordinate shrines |established= Traditionally attributed to the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}} |main_hall_style= [[Shinmei-zukuri]] |alternate_names= |sacred_sites= |annual_festival= |rituals= {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} }} '''Ōmizu Shrine''' (大水神社) is an auxiliary shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|zh|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}. This article also covers the subordinate shrines '''Kawaai Shrine''' (川相神社) and '''Kumabuchi Shrine''' (熊淵神社), which are co-located with Ōmizu Shrine. All three shrines are said to have been established by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}. Ōmizu Shrine enshrines a {{ill|Mountain God|ja|山の神|en}}, while Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine enshrine {{ill|water deities|ja|水神}}.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 == Overview == These three shrines are located at Uji Imazai-kechō, Hayasakiguchi 163-1, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, about 200 meters from the {{ill|Uji Bridge (Ise)|ja|宇治橋}} at the entrance to the Inner Shrine's sacred precinct.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 111 About 50 meters ahead is {{ill|Aedo Hashihime Shrine|ja|饗土橋姫神社}}, and 70 meters ahead are the auxiliary shrines {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社}}, {{ill|Shinkawa Shrine|ja|新川神社}}, and {{ill|Ishii Shrine|ja|石井神社}}, making a cluster of seven shrines.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): pp. 111, 116 The former {{ill|Hayasakibunko|ja|林崎文庫}}, predecessor of the {{ill|Jingū Library|ja|神宮文庫}}, is also located here.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 117 After ascending 12 stone steps, there is a {{ill|shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居}} and a single shrine building where all three shrines are co-located. An ancient camphor tree with a trunk diameter over 2 meters covers the shrine.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 The shrine, built in [[shinmei-zukuri]] style with plank roofing, faces south and is surrounded by a single {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣}} (sacred fence).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 245 The shrine measures approximately 1.8 m wide, 1.2 m deep, and 2.4 m high. No {{ill|saisenbako|ja|賽銭箱}} (offering box) is present. The shrine grounds cover 228 m² (approximately 69 tsubo).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 244 According to the ''Enryaku Gishikichō,'' the ancient precinct covered about 24,793 m² but has been reduced due to encroachments from private houses, temples, and the Hayasakibunko. Parts of the grounds on the north and east sides are lined with {{ill|stone walls|ja|石垣}}. == Ōmizu Shrine == Ōmizu Shrine is ranked 16th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–10 The enshrined deity is Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto (大山祇御祖命), sometimes written as "大山'''罪乃'''御祖命" in old texts.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 10Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 243 This deity is considered a child of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾命}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊弉冉命}}, worshipped as a mountain deity. Despite its name, meaning "great water shrine," the enshrined deity is a mountain god, which has puzzled some scholars. == Kawaai Shrine == Kawaai Shrine is ranked 10th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 13–14 The enshrined deity is Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami (細川水神), literally "narrow river water deity," a river god. == Kumabuchi Shrine == Kumabuchi Shrine is ranked 11th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine. The enshrined deity is Takiootogi-no-Kami (多支大刀自神), a guardian deity of sacred springs, considered a child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命). == History == According to the ''Jingū Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines Register'' issued in 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine was founded during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' records that a {{ill|shukube|ja|祝部}} was appointed to the shrine, with reconstruction handled by local officials. A record from 1482 confirms that the shrine still existed at that time. After falling into disrepair in the medieval period, the shrine was revived in 1664 under the leadership of {{ill|Kawabe no Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}}. In 1871, Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine were moved to share the same grounds as Ōmizu Shrine. These two shrines were originally located upstream along the {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}. == Rituals == The rituals at Ōmizu Shrine follow the same schedule as those at {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社}}. Ceremonies are held during the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} (June & December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} (October), and {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭}} (November).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 250 Other rituals, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭}} (January 3), National Foundation Day Festival (February 11), {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭}} (February 23), and {{ill|Kaze-no-hi-no-matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}} (May & August), are conducted remotely. == Flora == A 1977 survey of the shrine grounds identified 30 trees, including camphor, cedar, fig, chinquapin, and Japanese yew. == Access == ; To Uji Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} (JR) or {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}}. Get off at the "Naikū-mae" stop.Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Grand Shrine (Inner Shrine) Information]" (Accessed October 13, 2013) * Accessible from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} via Mie Kotsu bus. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} or {{ill|National Route 23|ja|国道23号}} from {{ill|Ise IC|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}}. Parking is available nearby. ; From Uji Bridge * About 5 minutes (240 m) on foot from Uji Bridge rotary and Naikū-mae parking lot. [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Water deities in Shinto]] [[ja:大水神社]] == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise Jingu|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧}} == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Ise Bunka-sha (ed.) ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Pilgrimage'' (Supplement to ''Isejin''), Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (ed.) ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1'', Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * {{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング}} ''Let's Go to Ise Jingu'', Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * {{ill|Shikinaisha Research Association|ja|式内社研究会}} (ed.) ''Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 6 Tōkaidō 1'', Kōgakkan University Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pages. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m01/0103.html Ōmizu Shrine (大水神社) - Co-located with Kawai Shrine and Kumafuchi Shrine] - Japan Ise Jingu Devotees Association Foundation [[Category:Shrines in Ise]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise Jingu]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines]] [[ja:大水神社]] {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:deprecated qq open]][[category:qq close]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:deprecated qq open]][[category:qq close]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ōmizu Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:大水神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]] [[ja:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[Category:New]]

This article also covers the subordinate shrines '''Kawaai Shrine''' (川相神社) and '''Kumabuchi Shrine''' (熊淵神社), which are co-located with Ōmizu Shrine. All three shrines are said to have been established by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}. Ōmizu Shrine enshrines a {{ill|Mountain God|ja|山の神|en}}, while Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine enshrine {{ill|water deities|ja|水神}}.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 == Overview == These three shrines are located at Uji Imazai-kechō, Hayasakiguchi 163-1, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, about 200 meters from the {{ill|Uji Bridge (Ise)|ja|宇治橋}} at the entrance to the Inner Shrine's sacred precinct.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 111 About 50 meters ahead is {{ill|Aedo Hashihime Shrine|ja|饗土橋姫神社}}, and 70 meters ahead are the auxiliary shrines {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社}}, {{ill|Shinkawa Shrine|ja|新川神社}}, and {{ill|Ishii Shrine|ja|石井神社}}, making a cluster of seven shrines.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): pp. 111, 116 The former {{ill|Hayasakibunko|ja|林崎文庫}}, predecessor of the {{ill|Jingū Library|ja|神宮文庫}}, is also located here.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 117 After ascending 12 stone steps, there is a {{ill|shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居}} and a single shrine building where all three shrines are co-located. An ancient camphor tree with a trunk diameter over 2 meters covers the shrine.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 The shrine, built in [[shinmei-zukuri]] style with plank roofing, faces south and is surrounded by a single {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣}} (sacred fence).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 245 The shrine measures approximately 1.8 m wide, 1.2 m deep, and 2.4 m high. No {{ill|saisenbako|ja|賽銭箱}} (offering box) is present. The shrine grounds cover 228 m² (approximately 69 tsubo).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 244 According to the ''Enryaku Gishikichō,'' the ancient precinct covered about 24,793 m² but has been reduced due to encroachments from private houses, temples, and the Hayasakibunko. Parts of the grounds on the north and east sides are lined with {{ill|stone walls|ja|石垣}}. == Ōmizu Shrine == Ōmizu Shrine is ranked 16th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–10 The enshrined deity is Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto (大山祇御祖命), sometimes written as "大山'''罪乃'''御祖命" in old texts.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 10Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 243 This deity is considered a child of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾命}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊弉冉命}}, worshipped as a mountain deity. Despite its name, meaning "great water shrine," the enshrined deity is a mountain god, which has puzzled some scholars. == Kawaai Shrine == Kawaai Shrine is ranked 10th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 13–14 The enshrined deity is Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami (細川水神), literally "narrow river water deity," a river god. == Kumabuchi Shrine == Kumabuchi Shrine is ranked 11th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine. The enshrined deity is Takiootogi-no-Kami (多支大刀自神), a guardian deity of sacred springs, considered a child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命). == History == According to the ''Jingū Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines Register'' issued in 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine was founded during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' records that a {{ill|shukube|ja|祝部}} was appointed to the shrine, with reconstruction handled by local officials. A record from 1482 confirms that the shrine still existed at that time. After falling into disrepair in the medieval period, the shrine was revived in 1664 under the leadership of {{ill|Kawabe no Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}}. In 1871, Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine were moved to share the same grounds as Ōmizu Shrine. These two shrines were originally located upstream along the {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}. == Rituals == The rituals at Ōmizu Shrine follow the same schedule as those at {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社}}. Ceremonies are held during the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} (June & December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} (October), and {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭}} (November).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 250 Other rituals, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭}} (January 3), National Foundation Day Festival (February 11), {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭}} (February 23), and {{ill|Kaze-no-hi-no-matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}} (May & August), are conducted remotely. == Flora == A 1977 survey of the shrine grounds identified 30 trees, including camphor, cedar, fig, chinquapin, and Japanese yew. == Access == ; To Uji Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} (JR) or {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}}. Get off at the "Naikū-mae" stop.Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Grand Shrine (Inner Shrine) Information]" (Accessed October 13, 2013) * Accessible from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} via Mie Kotsu bus. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} or {{ill|National Route 23|ja|国道23号}} from {{ill|Ise IC|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}}. Parking is available nearby. ; From Uji Bridge * About 5 minutes (240 m) on foot from Uji Bridge rotary and Naikū-mae parking lot. [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Water deities in Shinto]] [[ja:大水神社]] == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise Jingu|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧}} == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Ise Bunka-sha (ed.) ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Pilgrimage'' (Supplement to ''Isejin''), Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (ed.) ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1'', Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * {{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング}} ''Let's Go to Ise Jingu'', Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * {{ill|Shikinaisha Research Association|ja|式内社研究会}} (ed.) ''Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 6 Tōkaidō 1'', Kōgakkan University Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pages. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m01/0103.html Ōmizu Shrine (大水神社) - Co-located with Kawai Shrine and Kumafuchi Shrine] - Japan Ise Jingu Devotees Association Foundation [[Category:Shrines in Ise]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise Jingu]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines]] [[ja:大水神社]] {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ōmizu Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ōmizu Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ōmizu Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:大水神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]] [[ja:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[Category:New]]

The enshrined deity is Takiootogi-no-Kami (多支大刀自神), a guardian deity of sacred springs, considered a child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命). == History == According to the ''Jingū Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines Register'' issued in 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine was founded during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' records that a {{ill|shukube|ja|祝部}} was appointed to the shrine, with reconstruction handled by local officials. A record from 1482 confirms that the shrine still existed at that time. After falling into disrepair in the medieval period, the shrine was revived in 1664 under the leadership of {{ill|Kawabe no Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}}. In 1871, Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine were moved to share the same grounds as Ōmizu Shrine. These two shrines were originally located upstream along the {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}. == Rituals == The rituals at Ōmizu Shrine follow the same schedule as those at {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社}}. Ceremonies are held during the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} (June & December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} (October), and {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭}} (November).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 250 Other rituals, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭}} (January 3), National Foundation Day Festival (February 11), {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭}} (February 23), and {{ill|Kaze-no-hi-no-matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}} (May & August), are conducted remotely. == Flora == A 1977 survey of the shrine grounds identified 30 trees, including camphor, cedar, fig, chinquapin, and Japanese yew. == Access == ; To Uji Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} (JR) or {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}}. Get off at the "Naikū-mae" stop.Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Grand Shrine (Inner Shrine) Information]" (Accessed October 13, 2013) * Accessible from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} via Mie Kotsu bus. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} or {{ill|National Route 23|ja|国道23号}} from {{ill|Ise IC|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}}. Parking is available nearby. ; From Uji Bridge * About 5 minutes (240 m) on foot from Uji Bridge rotary and Naikū-mae parking lot.
