Get off at the "Naikū-mae" stop.Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Grand Shrine (Inner Shrine) Information]" (Accessed October 13, 2013) * Accessible from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|12=draft|12=draft}} via Mie Kotsu bus. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|12=draft|12=draft}} from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|12=draft|12=draft}} or {{ill|National Route 23|ja|国道23号|12=draft|12=draft}} from {{ill|Ise IC|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|12=draft|12=draft}}. Parking is available nearby. ; From Uji Bridge * About 5 minutes (240 m) on foot from Uji Bridge rotary and Naikū-mae parking lot. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise Jingu|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|12=draft|12=draft}} == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Ise Bunka-sha (ed.) ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Pilgrimage'' (Supplement to ''Isejin''), Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (ed.) ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1'', Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * {{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング|12=draft|12=draft}} ''Let's Go to Ise Jingu'', Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔|12=draft|12=draft}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * {{ill|Shikinaisha Research Association|ja|式内社研究会|12=draft|12=draft}} (ed.) ''Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 6 Tōkaidō 1'', Kōgakkan University Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pages. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m01/0103.html Ōmizu Shrine (大水神社) - Co-located with Kawai Shrine and Kumafuchi Shrine] - Japan Ise Jingu Devotees Association Foundation {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Water deities in Shinto]] [[Category:Shrines in Ise]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise Jingu]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines]] [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:大水神社]] [[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[ja:Ōmizu Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ōmizu Shrine}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "宇治山田駅" to "Ujiyamada Station"
revision.text:
{{infobox Shinto shrine |name= Ōmizu Shrine, Kawaai Shrine, Kumabuchi Shrine |image= [[File:Ōmizu-jinja 01.jpg|270px]] |location= {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Uji Imazai-kechō, Hayasakiguchi 163-1, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft|12=draft}} |latitude_deg = 34 |latitude_min = 27 |latitude_sec = 33.8 |longitude_deg = 136 |longitude_min = 43 |longitude_sec = 17.5 |map= Japan Mie |enshrined_deity= Ōmizu Shrine: Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto
Kawaai Shrine: Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami
Kumabuchi Shrine: Takiootogi-no-Kami |status= Ōmizu Shrine: {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft|12=draft}} (minor), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} auxiliary shrine
Kawaai Shrine & Kumabuchi Shrine: Kōtai Jingū subordinate shrines |established= Traditionally attributed to the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|12=draft|12=draft}} |main_hall_style= [[Shinmei-zukuri]] |alternate_names= |sacred_sites= |annual_festival= |rituals= {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|12=draft|12=draft}} }} '''Ōmizu Shrine''' (大水神社) is an auxiliary shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|zh|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft|12=draft}}. This article also covers the subordinate shrines '''Kawaai Shrine''' (川相神社) and '''Kumabuchi Shrine''' (熊淵神社), which are co-located with Ōmizu Shrine. All three shrines are said to have been established by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|12=draft|12=draft}}. Ōmizu Shrine enshrines a {{ill|Mountain God|ja|山の神|en|12=draft|12=draft}}, while Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine enshrine {{ill|water deities|ja|水神|1=Suijin|lt=water god|12=draft|12=draft}}.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 == Overview == These three shrines are located at Uji Imazai-kechō, Hayasakiguchi 163-1, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|12=draft|12=draft}}, about 200 meters from the {{ill|Uji Bridge (Ise)|ja|宇治橋|12=draft|12=draft}} at the entrance to the Inner Shrine's sacred precinct.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 111 About 50 meters ahead is {{ill|Aedo Hashihime Shrine|ja|饗土橋姫神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, and 70 meters ahead are the auxiliary shrines {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shinkawa Shrine|ja|新川神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Ishii Shrine|ja|石井神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, making a cluster of seven shrines.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): pp. 111, 116 The former {{ill|Hayasakibunko|ja|林崎文庫|12=draft|12=draft}}, predecessor of the {{ill|Jingū Library|ja|神宮文庫|12=draft|12=draft}}, is also located here.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 117 After ascending 12 stone steps, there is a {{ill|shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居|12=draft|12=draft}} and a single shrine building where all three shrines are co-located. An ancient camphor tree with a trunk diameter over 2 meters covers the shrine.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 The shrine, built in [[shinmei-zukuri]] style with plank roofing, faces south and is surrounded by a single {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣|12=draft|12=draft}} (sacred fence).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 245 The shrine measures approximately 1.8 m wide, 1.2 m deep, and 2.4 m high. No {{ill|saisenbako|ja|賽銭箱|12=draft|12=draft}} (offering box) is present. The shrine grounds cover 228 m² (approximately 69 tsubo).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 244 According to the ''Enryaku Gishikichō,'' the ancient precinct covered about 24,793 m² but has been reduced due to encroachments from private houses, temples, and the Hayasakibunko. Parts of the grounds on the north and east sides are lined with {{ill|stone walls|ja|石垣|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Ōmizu Shrine == Ōmizu Shrine is Ranked 16th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–10 The enshrined deity is Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto (大山祇御祖命), sometimes written as "大山'''罪乃'''御祖命" in old texts.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 10Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 243 This deity is considered a child of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾命|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊弉冉命|12=draft|12=draft}}, worshipped as a mountain deity. Despite its name, meaning "great water shrine," the enshrined deity is a mountain god, which has puzzled some scholars. == Kawaai Shrine == Kawaai Shrine is Ranked 10th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 13–14 The enshrined deity is Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami (細川水神), literally "narrow river water deity," a river god. == Kumabuchi Shrine == Kumabuchi Shrine is Ranked 11th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine. The enshrined deity is Takiootogi-no-Kami (多支大刀自神), a guardian deity of sacred springs, considered a child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命). == History == According to the ''Jingū Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines Register'' issued in 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine was founded during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}. The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' records that a {{ill|shukube|ja|祝部|12=draft|12=draft}} was appointed to the shrine, with reconstruction handled by local officials. A record from 1482 confirms that the shrine still existed at that time. After falling into disrepair in the medieval period, the shrine was revived in 1664 under the leadership of {{ill|Kawabe no Masanaga|ja|河邊精長|12=draft|12=draft}}. In 1871, Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine were moved to share the same grounds as Ōmizu Shrine. These two shrines were originally located upstream along the {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Rituals == The rituals at Ōmizu Shrine follow the same schedule as those at {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社|12=draft|12=draft}}. Ceremonies are held during the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭|12=draft|12=draft}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=draft|12=draft}} (June & December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|12=draft|12=draft}} (October), and {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭|12=draft|12=draft}} (November).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 250 Other rituals, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|12=draft|12=draft}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭|12=draft|12=draft}} (January 3), National Foundation Day Festival (February 11), {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭|12=draft|12=draft}} (February 23), and {{ill|Kaze-no-hi-no-matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|12=draft|12=draft}} (May & August), are conducted remotely. == Flora == A 1977 survey of the shrine grounds identified 30 trees, including camphor, cedar, fig, chinquapin, and Japanese yew. == Access == ; To Uji Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft|12=draft}} bus from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|12=draft|12=draft}} (JR) or {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|Ujiyamada Station|12=draft|12=draft}}. Get off at the "Naikū-mae" stop.Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Grand Shrine (Inner Shrine) Information]" (Accessed October 13, 2013) * Accessible from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|12=draft|12=draft}} via Mie Kotsu bus. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|12=draft|12=draft}} from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|12=draft|12=draft}} or {{ill|National Route 23|ja|国道23号|12=draft|12=draft}} from {{ill|Ise IC|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|12=draft|12=draft}}. Parking is available nearby. ; From Uji Bridge * About 5 minutes (240 m) on foot from Uji Bridge rotary and Naikū-mae parking lot. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise Jingu|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|12=draft|12=draft}} == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Ise Bunka-sha (ed.) ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Pilgrimage'' (Supplement to ''Isejin''), Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (ed.) ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1'', Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * {{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング|12=draft|12=draft}} ''Let's Go to Ise Jingu'', Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔|12=draft|12=draft}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * {{ill|Shikinaisha Research Association|ja|式内社研究会|12=draft|12=draft}} (ed.) ''Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 6 Tōkaidō 1'', Kōgakkan University Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pages. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m01/0103.html Ōmizu Shrine (大水神社) - Co-located with Kawai Shrine and Kumafuchi Shrine] - Japan Ise Jingu Devotees Association Foundation {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Water deities in Shinto]] [[Category:Shrines in Ise]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise Jingu]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines]] [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:大水神社]] [[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[ja:Ōmizu Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ōmizu Shrine}}

ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * {{ill|Shikinaisha Research Association|ja|式内社研究会|12=draft|12=draft}} (ed.) ''Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 6 Tōkaidō 1'', Kōgakkan University Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pages. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m01/0103.html Ōmizu Shrine (大水神社) - Co-located with Kawai Shrine and Kumafuchi Shrine] - Japan Ise Jingu Devotees Association Foundation {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Water deities in Shinto]] [[Category:Shrines in Ise]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines]] [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:大水神社]] [[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[ja:Ōmizu Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ōmizu Shrine}}

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:摂末社]] to [[Category:Setsumatsusha]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{infobox Shinto shrine |name= Ōmizu Shrine, Kawaai Shrine, Kumabuchi Shrine |image= [[File:Ōmizu-jinja 01.jpg|270px]] |location= {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Uji Imazai-kechō, Hayasakiguchi 163-1, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft|12=draft}} |latitude_deg = 34 |latitude_min = 27 |latitude_sec = 33.8 |longitude_deg = 136 |longitude_min = 43 |longitude_sec = 17.5 |map= Japan Mie |enshrined_deity= Ōmizu Shrine: Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto
Kawaai Shrine: Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami
Kumabuchi Shrine: Takiootogi-no-Kami |status= Ōmizu Shrine: {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft|12=draft}} (minor), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} auxiliary shrine
Kawaai Shrine & Kumabuchi Shrine: Kōtai Jingū subordinate shrines |established= Traditionally attributed to the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|12=draft|12=draft}} |main_hall_style= [[Shinmei-zukuri]] |alternate_names= |sacred_sites= |annual_festival= |rituals= {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|12=draft|12=draft}} }} '''Ōmizu Shrine''' (大水神社) is an auxiliary shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|zh|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft|12=draft}}. This article also covers the subordinate shrines '''Kawaai Shrine''' (川相神社) and '''Kumabuchi Shrine''' (熊淵神社), which are co-located with Ōmizu Shrine. All three shrines are said to have been established by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|12=draft|12=draft}}. Ōmizu Shrine enshrines a {{ill|Mountain God|ja|山の神|en|12=draft|12=draft}}, while Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine enshrine {{ill|water deities|ja|水神|1=Suijin|lt=water god|12=draft|12=draft}}.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 == Overview == These three shrines are located at Uji Imazai-kechō, Hayasakiguchi 163-1, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|12=draft|12=draft}}, about 200 meters from the {{ill|Uji Bridge (Ise)|ja|宇治橋|12=draft|12=draft}} at the entrance to the Inner Shrine's sacred precinct.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 111 About 50 meters ahead is {{ill|Aedo Hashihime Shrine|ja|饗土橋姫神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, and 70 meters ahead are the auxiliary shrines {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shinkawa Shrine|ja|新川神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Ishii Shrine|ja|石井神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, making a cluster of seven shrines.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): pp. 111, 116 The former {{ill|Hayasakibunko|ja|林崎文庫|12=draft|12=draft}}, predecessor of the {{ill|Jingū Library|ja|神宮文庫|12=draft|12=draft}}, is also located here.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 117 After ascending 12 stone steps, there is a {{ill|shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居|12=draft|12=draft}} and a single shrine building where all three shrines are co-located. An ancient camphor tree with a trunk diameter over 2 meters covers the shrine.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 The shrine, built in [[shinmei-zukuri]] style with plank roofing, faces south and is surrounded by a single {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣|12=draft|12=draft}} (sacred fence).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 245 The shrine measures approximately 1.8 m wide, 1.2 m deep, and 2.4 m high. No {{ill|saisenbako|ja|賽銭箱|12=draft|12=draft}} (offering box) is present. The shrine grounds cover 228 m² (approximately 69 tsubo).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 244 According to the ''Enryaku Gishikichō,'' the ancient precinct covered about 24,793 m² but has been reduced due to encroachments from private houses, temples, and the Hayasakibunko. Parts of the grounds on the north and east sides are lined with {{ill|stone walls|ja|石垣|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Ōmizu Shrine == Ōmizu Shrine is Ranked 16th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–10 The enshrined deity is Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto (大山祇御祖命), sometimes written as "大山'''罪乃'''御祖命" in old texts.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 10Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 243 This deity is considered a child of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾命|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊弉冉命|12=draft|12=draft}}, worshipped as a mountain deity. Despite its name, meaning "great water shrine," the enshrined deity is a mountain god, which has puzzled some scholars. == Kawaai Shrine == Kawaai Shrine is Ranked 10th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 13–14 The enshrined deity is Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami (細川水神), literally "narrow river water deity," a river god. == Kumabuchi Shrine == Kumabuchi Shrine is Ranked 11th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine. The enshrined deity is Takiootogi-no-Kami (多支大刀自神), a guardian deity of sacred springs, considered a child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命). == History == According to the ''Jingū Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines Register'' issued in 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine was founded during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}. The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' records that a {{ill|shukube|ja|祝部|12=draft|12=draft}} was appointed to the shrine, with reconstruction handled by local officials. A record from 1482 confirms that the shrine still existed at that time. After falling into disrepair in the medieval period, the shrine was revived in 1664 under the leadership of {{ill|Kawabe no Masanaga|ja|河邊精長|12=draft|12=draft}}. In 1871, Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine were moved to share the same grounds as Ōmizu Shrine. These two shrines were originally located upstream along the {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Rituals == The rituals at Ōmizu Shrine follow the same schedule as those at {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社|12=draft|12=draft}}. Ceremonies are held during the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭|12=draft|12=draft}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=draft|12=draft}} (June & December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|12=draft|12=draft}} (October), and {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭|12=draft|12=draft}} (November).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 250 Other rituals, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|12=draft|12=draft}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭|12=draft|12=draft}} (January 3), National Foundation Day Festival (February 11), {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭|12=draft|12=draft}} (February 23), and {{ill|Kaze-no-hi-no-matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|12=draft|12=draft}} (May & August), are conducted remotely. == Flora == A 1977 survey of the shrine grounds identified 30 trees, including camphor, cedar, fig, chinquapin, and Japanese yew. == Access == ; To Uji Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft|12=draft}} bus from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|12=draft|12=draft}} (JR) or {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|Ujiyamada Station|12=draft|12=draft}}. Get off at the "Naikū-mae" stop.Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Grand Shrine (Inner Shrine) Information]" (Accessed October 13, 2013) * Accessible from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|12=draft|12=draft}} via Mie Kotsu bus. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|12=draft|12=draft}} from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|12=draft|12=draft}} or {{ill|National Route 23|ja|国道23号|12=draft|12=draft}} from {{ill|Ise IC|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|12=draft|12=draft}}. Parking is available nearby. ; From Uji Bridge * About 5 minutes (240 m) on foot from Uji Bridge rotary and Naikū-mae parking lot. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise Jingu|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|12=draft|12=draft}} == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Ise Bunka-sha (ed.) ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Pilgrimage'' (Supplement to ''Isejin''), Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (ed.) ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1'', Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * {{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング|12=draft|12=draft}} ''Let's Go to Ise Jingu'', Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔|12=draft|12=draft}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * {{ill|Shikinaisha Research Association|ja|式内社研究会|12=draft|12=draft}} (ed.) ''Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 6 Tōkaidō 1'', Kōgakkan University Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pages. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m01/0103.html Ōmizu Shrine (大水神社) - Co-located with Kawai Shrine and Kumafuchi Shrine] - Japan Ise Jingu Devotees Association Foundation {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Water deities in Shinto]] [[Category:Shrines in Ise]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines]] [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:Setsumatsusha]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:大水神社]] [[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[ja:Ōmizu Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ōmizu Shrine}}

{{infobox Shinto shrine |name= Ōmizu Shrine, Kawaai Shrine, Kumabuchi Shrine |image= [[File:Ōmizu-jinja 01.jpg|270px]] |location= {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Uji Imazai-kechō, Hayasakiguchi 163-1, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft|12=draft}} |latitude_deg = 34 |latitude_min = 27 |latitude_sec = 33.8 |longitude_deg = 136 |longitude_min = 43 |longitude_sec = 17.5 |map= Japan Mie |enshrined_deity= Ōmizu Shrine: Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto
Kawaai Shrine: Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami
Kumabuchi Shrine: Takiootogi-no-Kami |status= Ōmizu Shrine: {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft|12=draft}} (minor), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} auxiliary shrine
Kawaai Shrine & Kumabuchi Shrine: Kōtai Jingū subordinate shrines |established= Traditionally attributed to the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|12=draft|12=draft}} |main_hall_style= [[Shinmei-zukuri]] |alternate_names= |sacred_sites= |annual_festival= |rituals= {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|12=draft|12=draft}} }} '''Ōmizu Shrine''' (大水神社) is an auxiliary shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|zh|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft|12=draft}}. This article also covers the subordinate shrines '''Kawaai Shrine''' (川相神社) and '''Kumabuchi Shrine''' (熊淵神社), which are co-located with Ōmizu Shrine. All three shrines are said to have been established by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|12=draft|12=draft}}. Ōmizu Shrine enshrines a {{ill|Mountain God|ja|山の神|en|12=draft|12=draft}}, while Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine enshrine {{ill|water deities|ja|水神|1=Suijin|lt=water god|12=draft|12=draft}}.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 == Overview == These three shrines are located at Uji Imazai-kechō, Hayasakiguchi 163-1, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|12=draft|12=draft}}, about 200 meters from the {{ill|Uji Bridge (Ise)|ja|宇治橋|12=draft|12=draft}} at the entrance to the Inner Shrine's sacred precinct.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 111 About 50 meters ahead is {{ill|Aedo Hashihime Shrine|ja|饗土橋姫神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, and 70 meters ahead are the auxiliary shrines {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shinkawa Shrine|ja|新川神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Ishii Shrine|ja|石井神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, making a cluster of seven shrines.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): pp. 111, 116 The former {{ill|Hayasakibunko|ja|林崎文庫|12=draft|12=draft}}, predecessor of the {{ill|Jingū Library|ja|神宮文庫|12=draft|12=draft}}, is also located here.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 117 After ascending 12 stone steps, there is a {{ill|shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居|12=draft|12=draft}} and a single shrine building where all three shrines are co-located. An ancient camphor tree with a trunk diameter over 2 meters covers the shrine.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 The shrine, built in [[shinmei-zukuri]] style with plank roofing, faces south and is surrounded by a single {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣|12=draft|12=draft}} (sacred fence).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 245 The shrine measures approximately 1.8 m wide, 1.2 m deep, and 2.4 m high. No {{ill|saisenbako|ja|賽銭箱|12=draft|12=draft}} (offering box) is present. The shrine grounds cover 228 m² (approximately 69 tsubo).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 244 According to the ''Enryaku Gishikichō,'' the ancient precinct covered about 24,793 m² but has been reduced due to encroachments from private houses, temples, and the Hayasakibunko. Parts of the grounds on the north and east sides are lined with {{ill|stone walls|ja|石垣|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Ōmizu Shrine == Ōmizu Shrine is Ranked 16th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–10 The enshrined deity is Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto (大山祇御祖命), sometimes written as "大山'''罪乃'''御祖命" in old texts.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 10Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 243 This deity is considered a child of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾命|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊弉冉命|12=draft|12=draft}}, worshipped as a mountain deity. Despite its name, meaning "great water shrine," the enshrined deity is a mountain god, which has puzzled some scholars. == Kawaai Shrine == Kawaai Shrine is Ranked 10th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 13–14 The enshrined deity is Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami (細川水神), literally "narrow river water deity," a river god. == Kumabuchi Shrine == Kumabuchi Shrine is Ranked 11th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine. The enshrined deity is Takiootogi-no-Kami (多支大刀自神), a guardian deity of sacred springs, considered a child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命). == History == According to the ''Jingū Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines Register'' issued in 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine was founded during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}. The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' records that a {{ill|shukube|ja|祝部|12=draft|12=draft}} was appointed to the shrine, with reconstruction handled by local officials. A record from 1482 confirms that the shrine still existed at that time. After falling into disrepair in the medieval period, the shrine was revived in 1664 under the leadership of {{ill|Kawabe no Masanaga|ja|河邊精長|12=draft|12=draft}}. In 1871, Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine were moved to share the same grounds as Ōmizu Shrine. These two shrines were originally located upstream along the {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Rituals == The rituals at Ōmizu Shrine follow the same schedule as those at {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社|12=draft|12=draft}}. Ceremonies are held during the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭|12=draft|12=draft}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=draft|12=draft}} (June & December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|12=draft|12=draft}} (October), and {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭|12=draft|12=draft}} (November).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 250 Other rituals, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|12=draft|12=draft}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭|12=draft|12=draft}} (January 3), National Foundation Day Festival (February 11), {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭|12=draft|12=draft}} (February 23), and {{ill|Kaze-no-hi-no-matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|12=draft|12=draft}} (May & August), are conducted remotely. == Flora == A 1977 survey of the shrine grounds identified 30 trees, including camphor, cedar, fig, chinquapin, and Japanese yew. == Access == ; To Uji Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft|12=draft}} bus from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|12=draft|12=draft}} (JR) or {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|Ujiyamada Station|12=draft|12=draft}}. Get off at the "Naikū-mae" stop.Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Grand Shrine (Inner Shrine) Information]" (Accessed October 13, 2013) * Accessible from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|12=draft|12=draft}} via Mie Kotsu bus. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|12=draft|12=draft}} from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|12=draft|12=draft}} or {{ill|National Route 23|ja|国道23号|12=draft|12=draft}} from {{ill|Ise IC|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|12=draft|12=draft}}. Parking is available nearby. ; From Uji Bridge * About 5 minutes (240 m) on foot from Uji Bridge rotary and Naikū-mae parking lot. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise Jingu|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|12=draft|12=draft}} == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Ise Bunka-sha (ed.) ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Pilgrimage'' (Supplement to ''Isejin''), Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (ed.) ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1'', Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * {{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング|12=draft|12=draft}} ''Let's Go to Ise Jingu'', Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔|12=draft|12=draft}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * {{ill|Shikinaisha Research Association|ja|式内社研究会|12=draft|12=draft}} (ed.) ''Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 6 Tōkaidō 1'', Kōgakkan University Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pages. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m01/0103.html Ōmizu Shrine (大水神社) - Co-located with Kawai Shrine and Kumafuchi Shrine] - Japan Ise Jingu Devotees Association Foundation {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Water deities in Shinto]] [[Category:Shrines in Ise]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Ise City]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[Category:Setsumatsusha]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:大水神社]] [[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[ja:Ōmizu Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ōmizu Shrine}}

revision.comment: 14 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:大水神社 up to rev 95619690
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|伊勢神宮皇大神宮（内宮）の摂社}} {{神社 |名称= 大水神社・川相神社・熊淵神社 |画像= [[File:Ōmizu-jinja 01.jpg|270px]] |所在地= [[三重県]][[伊勢市]]宇治今在家町字林崎163-1 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 33.8 |経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 43 | 経度秒 = 17.5 |地図= Japan Mie |祭神= 大水神社：大山祇御祖命
川相神社：細川水神
熊淵神社：多支大刀自神 |社格= 大水神社：式内社（小）、[[皇大神宮]]摂社
川相神社・熊淵神社：皇大神宮末社 |創建= 伝[[垂仁天皇]]御世 |本殿= [[神明造]] |別名= |札所等= |例祭= |神事= [[神嘗祭]] }} '''大水神社'''（おおみずじんじゃ）は、[[伊勢神宮]][[皇大神宮]]（内宮）の[[摂末社|摂社]]。本項目では、大水神社と同座する、内宮末社の'''川相神社'''（かわあいじんじゃ）および'''熊淵神社'''（くまぶちじんじゃ）についても記述する。 3社とも[[倭姫命]]が定めた[[神社]]であるといい、大水神社は[[山の神]]を、川相神社・熊淵神社は[[水神|水の神]]を祀る学研パブリッシング（2013）：54 - 55ページ。 == 概要 == ここでは3社共通事項を記述する。 [[三重県]][[伊勢市]]宇治今在家町字林崎163-1式内社研究会 編（1990）：242ページ、内宮の神域入り口に架かる[[宇治橋 (伊勢市)|宇治橋]]から約200[[メートル|m]]のところに鎮座する伊勢文化舎（2008）：111ページ。大水神社から50[[メートル|m]]先には内宮所管社の[[饗土橋姫神社]]（あえどはしひめじんじゃ）、70m先には内宮摂末社の[[津長神社|津長神社・新川神社・石井神社]]があり、7社が集まっている伊勢文化舎（2008）：111, 116ページ。また[[神宮文庫]]の前身である旧林崎文庫もこの地にある伊勢文化舎（2008）：117ページ。 12段の石段を上ると、[[神明鳥居]]1基と[[社殿]]がある。3社は同座しており、社殿は1つである。枝分かれした大楠の古木が社殿の上を覆っている学研パブリッシング（2013）：54 - 55ページ。この大楠は直径2m以上ある式内社研究会 編（1990）：245ページ。[[神明造]][[板葺]]の社殿は[[南]]を向いており、一重の[[玉垣]]に囲まれている。社殿の大きさは、幅6尺（約1.8m）、奥行き4尺（約1.2m）、高さ7尺8寸（約2.4m）。[[賽銭]]箱は置かれていない。 社地の面積は68.97[[坪]]（228[[平方メートル|m2]]）式内社研究会 編（1990）：244ページ。『延暦儀式帳』によると、古代の社地は2町5反（約24,793[[平方メートル|m2]]）と大変広かったが、その後社地の一部が[[住宅|民家]]や[[寺院]]、林崎文庫の敷地になるなどして縮小したと見られる。社地北側と東側には一部[[石垣]]が築かれている。 == 大水神社 == 大水神社は、内宮の摂社27社のうち第16位である宇治山田市役所 編（1929）：9 - 10ページ。 [[祭神]]は大山祇御祖命（おおやまづみのみおやのみこと）。「大山'''罪乃'''御祖命」と表記する文献もあり宇治山田市役所 編（1929）：10ページ式内社研究会 編（1990）：243ページ、古書ではこの名で一致する。[[イザナギ|伊弉諾命]]（いざなぎのみこと）・[[イザナミ|伊弉冉命]]（いざなみのみこと）の子であり、[[山の神|山神]]だとされる。 社名が「大水」であるのに祭神が「山の神」であることをいぶかしがる文献もある。 == 川相神社 == 川相神社は、内宮の末社16社のうち、第10位である宇治山田市役所 編（1929）：13 - 14ページ。 祭神は細川水神（ほそかわのみずのかみ）。その名の通り、川の神である。 == 熊淵神社 == 熊淵神社は、内宮の末社16社のうち、第11位である。 祭神は多支大刀自神（たきおおとじのかみ）。大水上命（おおみなかみのみこと）の子で、石清水の[[守護神]]である。 == 歴史 == [[1880年]]（明治13年）発行の『神宮摂末社以下明細取調帳』によると、創建は[[垂仁天皇]]の御代とされる。『皇大神宮儀式帳』の記載によると、大水神社には[[祝部]]が任じられており、社殿の造り替えは[[国司|国]][[郡司]]が担った。『氏経神事記』の[[文明 (日本)|文明]]14年[[1月15日 (旧暦)|正月15日]]（[[ユリウス暦]]：[[1482年]][[2月3日]]）条には大水神社の祝部が神事に奉仕していた旨記述があることから、少なくともこの時代までは社殿が存在したことが確認されている。 [[中世]]以後祭祀の断絶により社殿は廃絶し、社地のみが残された。[[寛文]]3年[[12月21日 (旧暦)|12月21日]]（[[グレゴリオ暦]]：[[1664年]][[1月19日]]）に[[遷御]]（せんぎょ）の儀を行い再興された。再興したのは大宮司の[[河辺精長|河邊精長]]（大中臣精長）であり、以後宮司の責任で造り替えが行われるようになった。[[明治4年]]（[[1871年]]/[[1872年]]）、大水神社に川相神社・熊淵神社を同座させる。川相神社・熊淵神社は元来五十鈴川の上流に鎮座していた。[[1877年]]（明治10年）9月に職制改正に伴い、[[守衛]]が置かれるようになった。 社殿は[[1918年]]（大正7年）7月と[[1956年]]（昭和31年）2月に建て替えが行われ、[[1977年]]（昭和52年）[[3月24日]]に大修繕が完了した。 == 祭祀 == 大水神社で執行される祭祀は津長神社と同じである。すなわち、巡回祭典の形で[[祈年祭]]（2月）、[[月次祭]]（6月・12月）、[[神嘗祭]]（10月）、[[新嘗祭]]（11月）の際は社殿の前で祭祀が行われ、[[歳旦祭]]（1月1日）、[[元始祭]]（1月3日）、建国記念祭（[[2月11日]]）、[[天長祭]]（[[2月23日]]）、風日祈祭（5月・8月）は遥祀にて催行する式内社研究会 編（1990）：250ページ。 == 植物相 == 1977年（昭和52年）に行われた境内の[[立木]]調査によると、[[クス]]・[[スギ]]・[[イヌビワ]]・[[シイ]]・[[イヌマキ|マキ]]を主要樹種とし、計30本あった。 == 交通 == ;宇治橋前まで * [[東海旅客鉄道|JR]][[参宮線]]・[[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[近鉄山田線|山田線]][[伊勢市駅]]南口（JR側）または近鉄山田線・[[近鉄鳥羽線|鳥羽線]][[宇治山田駅]]より三重交通バスに15分ほど乗車、終点「内宮前」下車三重県観光連盟"[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html 伊勢神宮（内宮）の観光施設・周辺情報 - 観光三重]"（2013年10月13日閲覧。）。近鉄鳥羽線[[五十鈴川駅]]からも三重交通バスで内宮前へ行くことが可能である。 * [[伊勢自動車道]][[伊勢西インターチェンジ|伊勢西IC]]より[[三重県道32号伊勢磯部線]]（御木本道路）を南へ約5分または[[伊勢インターチェンジ|伊勢IC]]より[[国道23号]]を南へ約5分、付近に[[駐車場]]あり。 ;宇治橋前から * 宇治橋前ロータリー、内宮前駐車場を横切り、徒歩約5分（約240m）。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}} == 参考文献 == * [[伊勢文化舎]] 編『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、伊勢文化舎、平成20年12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4年1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013年7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[皇學館大学]]出版部、平成2年2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == 関連項目 == * [[神宮125社の一覧]] * [[伊勢国の式内社一覧]] == 外部リンク == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m01/0103.html 大水神社（おおみずじんじゃ） 御同座 川相神社・熊淵神社] - 財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会 {{神宮125社}} {{神道 横}} {{デフォルトソート:おおみすしんしや}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:神宮125社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]]

川相神社：細川水神
熊淵神社：多支大刀自神 |社格= 大水神社：式内社（小）、[[皇大神宮]]摂社

14ページ。 祭神は細川水神（ほそかわのみずのかみ）。その名の通り、川の神である。 == 熊淵神社 == 熊淵神社は、内宮の末社16社のうち、第11位である。 祭神は多支大刀自神（たきおおとじのかみ）。大水上命（おおみなかみのみこと）の子で、石清水の[[守護神]]である。 == 歴史 == [[1880年]]（明治13年）発行の『神宮摂末社以下明細取調帳』によると、創建は[[垂仁天皇]]の御代とされる。『皇大神宮儀式帳』の記載によると、大水神社には[[祝部]]が任じられており、社殿の造り替えは[[国司|国]][[郡司]]が担った。『氏経神事記』の[[文明 (日本)|文明]]14年[[1月15日 (旧暦)|正月15日]]（[[ユリウス暦]]：[[1482年]][[2月3日]]）条には大水神社の祝部が神事に奉仕していた旨記述があることから、少なくともこの時代までは社殿が存在したことが確認されている。 [[中世]]以後祭祀の断絶により社殿は廃絶し、社地のみが残された。[[寛文]]3年[[12月21日 (旧暦)|12月21日]]（[[グレゴリオ暦]]：[[1664年]][[1月19日]]）に[[遷御]]（せんぎょ）の儀を行い再興された。再興したのは大宮司の[[河辺精長|河邊精長]]（大中臣精長）であり、以後宮司の責任で造り替えが行われるようになった。[[明治4年]]（[[1871年]]/[[1872年]]）、大水神社に川相神社・熊淵神社を同座させる。川相神社・熊淵神社は元来五十鈴川の上流に鎮座していた。[[1877年]]（明治10年）9月に職制改正に伴い、[[守衛]]が置かれるようになった。 社殿は[[1918年]]（大正7年）7月と[[1956年]]（昭和31年）2月に建て替えが行われ、[[1977年]]（昭和52年）[[3月24日]]に大修繕が完了した。 == 祭祀 == 大水神社で執行される祭祀は津長神社と同じである。すなわち、巡回祭典の形で[[祈年祭]]（2月）、[[月次祭]]（6月・12月）、[[神嘗祭]]（10月）、[[新嘗祭]]（11月）の際は社殿の前で祭祀が行われ、[[歳旦祭]]（1月1日）、[[元始祭]]（1月3日）、建国記念祭（[[2月11日]]）、[[天長祭]]（[[2月23日]]）、風日祈祭（5月・8月）は遥祀にて催行する式内社研究会 編（1990）：250ページ。 == 植物相 == 1977年（昭和52年）に行われた境内の[[立木]]調査によると、[[クス]]・[[スギ]]・[[イヌビワ]]・[[シイ]]・[[イヌマキ|マキ]]を主要樹種とし、計30本あった。 == 交通 == ;宇治橋前まで * [[東海旅客鉄道|JR]][[参宮線]]・[[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[近鉄山田線|山田線]][[伊勢市駅]]南口（JR側）または近鉄山田線・[[近鉄鳥羽線|鳥羽線]][[宇治山田駅]]より三重交通バスに15分ほど乗車、終点「内宮前」下車三重県観光連盟"[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html 伊勢神宮（内宮）の観光施設・周辺情報 - 観光三重]"（2013年10月13日閲覧。）。近鉄鳥羽線[[五十鈴川駅]]からも三重交通バスで内宮前へ行くことが可能である。 * [[伊勢自動車道]][[伊勢西インターチェンジ|伊勢西IC]]より[[三重県道32号伊勢磯部線]]（御木本道路）を南へ約5分または[[伊勢インターチェンジ|伊勢IC]]より[[国道23号]]を南へ約5分、付近に[[駐車場]]あり。 ;宇治橋前から * 宇治橋前ロータリー、内宮前駐車場を横切り、徒歩約5分（約240m）。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}} == 参考文献 == * [[伊勢文化舎]] 編『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、伊勢文化舎、平成20年12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4年1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013年7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[皇學館大学]]出版部、平成2年2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == 関連項目 == * [[神宮125社の一覧]] * [[伊勢国の式内社一覧]] == 外部リンク == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m01/0103.html 大水神社（おおみずじんじゃ） 御同座 川相神社・熊淵神社] - 財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会 {{神宮125社}} {{神道 横}} {{デフォルトソート:おおみすしんしや}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:神宮125社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]]

revision.text:
{{infobox Shinto shrine |name= Ōmizu Shrine, Kawaai Shrine, Kumabuchi Shrine |image= [[File:Ōmizu-jinja 01.jpg|270px]] |location= {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Uji Imazai-kechō, Hayasakiguchi 163-1, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} |latitude_deg = 34 |latitude_min = 27 |latitude_sec = 33.8 |longitude_deg = 136 |longitude_min = 43 |longitude_sec = 17.5 |map= Japan Mie |enshrined_deity= Ōmizu Shrine: Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto
Kawaai Shrine: Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami
Kumabuchi Shrine: Takiootogi-no-Kami |status= Ōmizu Shrine: {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (minor), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} auxiliary shrine
Kawaai Shrine & Kumabuchi Shrine: Kōtai Jingū subordinate shrines |established= Traditionally attributed to the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} |main_hall_style= [[Shinmei-zukuri]] |alternate_names= |sacred_sites= |annual_festival= |rituals= {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} }} '''Ōmizu Shrine''' (大水神社) is an auxiliary shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|zh|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This article also covers the subordinate shrines '''Kawaai Shrine''' (川相神社) and '''Kumabuchi Shrine''' (熊淵神社), which are co-located with Ōmizu Shrine. All three shrines are said to have been established by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Ōmizu Shrine enshrines a {{ill|Mountain God|ja|山の神|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, while Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine enshrine {{ill|water deities|ja|水神|1=Suijin|lt=water god|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 == Overview == These three shrines are located at Uji Imazai-kechō, Hayasakiguchi 163-1, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, about 200 meters from the {{ill|Uji Bridge (Ise)|ja|宇治橋|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at the entrance to the Inner Shrine's sacred precinct.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 111 About 50 meters ahead is {{ill|Aedo Hashihime Shrine|ja|饗土橋姫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and 70 meters ahead are the auxiliary shrines {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shinkawa Shrine|ja|新川神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Ishii Shrine|ja|石井神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, making a cluster of seven shrines.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): pp. 111, 116 The former {{ill|Hayasakibunko|ja|林崎文庫|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, predecessor of the {{ill|Jingū Library|ja|神宮文庫|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, is also located here.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 117 After ascending 12 stone steps, there is a {{ill|shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and a single shrine building where all three shrines are co-located. An ancient camphor tree with a trunk diameter over 2 meters covers the shrine.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 The shrine, built in [[shinmei-zukuri]] style with plank roofing, faces south and is surrounded by a single {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (sacred fence).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 245 The shrine measures approximately 1.8 m wide, 1.2 m deep, and 2.4 m high. No {{ill|saisenbako|ja|賽銭箱|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (offering box) is present. The shrine grounds cover 228 m² (approximately 69 tsubo).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 244 According to the ''Enryaku Gishikichō,'' the ancient precinct covered about 24,793 m² but has been reduced due to encroachments from private houses, temples, and the Hayasakibunko. Parts of the grounds on the north and east sides are lined with {{ill|stone walls|ja|石垣|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Ōmizu Shrine == Ōmizu Shrine is Ranked 16th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–10 The enshrined deity is Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto (大山祇御祖命), sometimes written as "大山'''罪乃'''御祖命" in old texts.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 10Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 243 This deity is considered a child of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊弉冉命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, worshipped as a mountain deity. Despite its name, meaning "great water shrine," the enshrined deity is a mountain god, which has puzzled some scholars. == Kawaai Shrine == Kawaai Shrine is Ranked 10th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 13–14 The enshrined deity is Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami (細川水神), literally "narrow river water deity," a river god. == Kumabuchi Shrine == Kumabuchi Shrine is Ranked 11th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine. The enshrined deity is Takiootogi-no-Kami (多支大刀自神), a guardian deity of sacred springs, considered a child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命). == History == According to the ''Jingū Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines Register'' issued in 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine was founded during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' records that a {{ill|shukube|ja|祝部|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was appointed to the shrine, with reconstruction handled by local officials. A record from 1482 confirms that the shrine still existed at that time. After falling into disrepair in the medieval period, the shrine was revived in 1664 under the leadership of {{ill|Kawabe no Masanaga|ja|河邊精長|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In 1871, Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine were moved to share the same grounds as Ōmizu Shrine. These two shrines were originally located upstream along the {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Rituals == The rituals at Ōmizu Shrine follow the same schedule as those at {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Ceremonies are held during the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (June & December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (October), and {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (November).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 250 Other rituals, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (January 3), National Foundation Day Festival (February 11), {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (February 23), and {{ill|Kaze-no-hi-no-matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (May & August), are conducted remotely. == Flora == A 1977 survey of the shrine grounds identified 30 trees, including camphor, cedar, fig, chinquapin, and Japanese yew. == Access == ; To Uji Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|qq=draft}} bus from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (JR) or {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|Ujiyamada Station|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Get off at the "Naikū-mae" stop.Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Grand Shrine (Inner Shrine) Information]" (Accessed October 13, 2013) * Accessible from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} via Mie Kotsu bus. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or {{ill|National Route 23|ja|国道23号|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from {{ill|Ise IC|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Parking is available nearby. ; From Uji Bridge * About 5 minutes (240 m) on foot from Uji Bridge rotary and Naikū-mae parking lot. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise Jingu|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Ise Bunka-sha (ed.) ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Pilgrimage'' (Supplement to ''Isejin''), Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (ed.) ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1'', Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * {{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ''Let's Go to Ise Jingu'', Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * {{ill|Shikinaisha Research Association|ja|式内社研究会|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (ed.) ''Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 6 Tōkaidō 1'', Kōgakkan University Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pages. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m01/0103.html Ōmizu Shrine (大水神社) - Co-located with Kawai Shrine and Kumafuchi Shrine] - Japan Ise Jingu Devotees Association Foundation {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Water deities in Shinto]] [[Category:Shrines in Ise]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Ise City]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[Category:Setsumatsusha]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:大水神社]] [[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[ja:Ōmizu Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ōmizu Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|大水神社|version=95619690|comment=Imported full ja history}}

All three shrines are said to have been established by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Ōmizu Shrine enshrines a {{ill|Mountain God|ja|山の神|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, while Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine enshrine {{ill|water deities|ja|水神|1=Suijin|lt=water god|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 == Overview == These three shrines are located at Uji Imazai-kechō, Hayasakiguchi 163-1, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, about 200 meters from the {{ill|Uji Bridge (Ise)|ja|宇治橋|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at the entrance to the Inner Shrine's sacred precinct.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 111 About 50 meters ahead is {{ill|Aedo Hashihime Shrine|ja|饗土橋姫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and 70 meters ahead are the auxiliary shrines {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shinkawa Shrine|ja|新川神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Ishii Shrine|ja|石井神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, making a cluster of seven shrines.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): pp. 111, 116 The former {{ill|Hayasakibunko|ja|林崎文庫|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, predecessor of the {{ill|Jingū Library|ja|神宮文庫|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, is also located here.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 117 After ascending 12 stone steps, there is a {{ill|shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and a single shrine building where all three shrines are co-located. An ancient camphor tree with a trunk diameter over 2 meters covers the shrine.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 The shrine, built in [[shinmei-zukuri]] style with plank roofing, faces south and is surrounded by a single {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (sacred fence).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 245 The shrine measures approximately 1.8 m wide, 1.2 m deep, and 2.4 m high. No {{ill|saisenbako|ja|賽銭箱|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (offering box) is present. The shrine grounds cover 228 m² (approximately 69 tsubo).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 244 According to the ''Enryaku Gishikichō,'' the ancient precinct covered about 24,793 m² but has been reduced due to encroachments from private houses, temples, and the Hayasakibunko. Parts of the grounds on the north and east sides are lined with {{ill|stone walls|ja|石垣|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Ōmizu Shrine == Ōmizu Shrine is Ranked 16th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–10 The enshrined deity is Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto (大山祇御祖命), sometimes written as "大山'''罪乃'''御祖命" in old texts.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 10Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 243 This deity is considered a child of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊弉冉命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, worshipped as a mountain deity. Despite its name, meaning "great water shrine," the enshrined deity is a mountain god, which has puzzled some scholars. == Kawaai Shrine == Kawaai Shrine is Ranked 10th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 13–14 The enshrined deity is Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami (細川水神), literally "narrow river water deity," a river god. == Kumabuchi Shrine == Kumabuchi Shrine is Ranked 11th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine. The enshrined deity is Takiootogi-no-Kami (多支大刀自神), a guardian deity of sacred springs, considered a child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命). == History == According to the ''Jingū Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines Register'' issued in 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine was founded during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' records that a {{ill|shukube|ja|祝部|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was appointed to the shrine, with reconstruction handled by local officials. A record from 1482 confirms that the shrine still existed at that time. After falling into disrepair in the medieval period, the shrine was revived in 1664 under the leadership of {{ill|Kawabe no Masanaga|ja|河邊精長|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In 1871, Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine were moved to share the same grounds as Ōmizu Shrine. These two shrines were originally located upstream along the {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Rituals == The rituals at Ōmizu Shrine follow the same schedule as those at {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Ceremonies are held during the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (June & December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (October), and {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (November).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 250 Other rituals, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (January 3), National Foundation Day Festival (February 11), {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (February 23), and {{ill|Kaze-no-hi-no-matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (May & August), are conducted remotely. == Flora == A 1977 survey of the shrine grounds identified 30 trees, including camphor, cedar, fig, chinquapin, and Japanese yew. == Access == ; To Uji Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|qq=draft}} bus from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (JR) or {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|Ujiyamada Station|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Get off at the "Naikū-mae" stop.Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Grand Shrine (Inner Shrine) Information]" (Accessed October 13, 2013) * Accessible from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} via Mie Kotsu bus. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or {{ill|National Route 23|ja|国道23号|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from {{ill|Ise IC|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Parking is available nearby. ; From Uji Bridge * About 5 minutes (240 m) on foot from Uji Bridge rotary and Naikū-mae parking lot. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise Jingu|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Ise Bunka-sha (ed.) ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Pilgrimage'' (Supplement to ''Isejin''), Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (ed.) ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1'', Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * {{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ''Let's Go to Ise Jingu'', Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * {{ill|Shikinaisha Research Association|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft研究会|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (ed.) ''Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 6 Tōkaidō 1'', Kōgakkan University Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pages. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m01/0103.html Ōmizu Shrine (大水神社) - Co-located with Kawai Shrine and Kumafuchi Shrine] - Japan Ise Jingu Devotees Association Foundation {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ōmizu Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|大水神社|version=95619690|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Water deities in Shinto]] [[Category:Shrines in Ise]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Ise City]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[Category:Setsumatsusha]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:大水神社]] [[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[ja:Ōmizu Shrine]]

An ancient camphor tree with a trunk diameter over 2 meters covers the shrine.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 The shrine, built in [[shinmei-zukuri]] style with plank roofing, faces south and is surrounded by a single {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (sacred fence).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 245 The shrine measures approximately 1.8 m wide, 1.2 m deep, and 2.4 m high. No {{ill|saisenbako|ja|賽銭箱|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (offering box) is present. The shrine grounds cover 228 m² (approximately 69 tsubo).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 244 According to the ''Enryaku Gishikichō,'' the ancient precinct covered about 24,793 m² but has been reduced due to encroachments from private houses, temples, and the Hayasakibunko. Parts of the grounds on the north and east sides are lined with {{ill|stone walls|ja|石垣|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Ōmizu Shrine == Ōmizu Shrine is Ranked 16th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–10 The enshrined deity is Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto (大山祇御祖命), sometimes written as "大山'''罪乃'''御祖命" in old texts.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 10Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 243 This deity is considered a child of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊弉冉命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, worshipped as a mountain deity. Despite its name, meaning "great water shrine," the enshrined deity is a mountain god, which has puzzled some scholars. == Kawaai Shrine == Kawaai Shrine is Ranked 10th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 13–14 The enshrined deity is Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami (細川水神), literally "narrow river water deity," a river god. == Kumabuchi Shrine == Kumabuchi Shrine is Ranked 11th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine. The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' records that a {{ill|shukube|ja|祝部|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was appointed to the shrine, with reconstruction handled by local officials. In 1871, Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine were moved to share the same grounds as Ōmizu Shrine. Get off at the "Naikū-mae" stop.Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Grand Shrine (Inner Shrine) Information]" (Accessed October 13, 2013) * Accessible from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} via Mie Kotsu bus. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or {{ill|National Route 23|ja|国道23号|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from {{ill|Ise IC|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Parking is available nearby. ; From Uji Bridge * About 5 minutes (240 m) on foot from Uji Bridge rotary and Naikū-mae parking lot. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise Jingu|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Ise Bunka-sha (ed.) ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1'', Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * {{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ''Let's Go to Ise Jingu'', Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * {{ill|Shikinaisha Research Association|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft研究会|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (ed.) ''Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 6 Tōkaidō 1'', Kōgakkan University Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pages. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m01/0103.html Ōmizu Shrine (大水神社) - Co-located with Kawai Shrine and Kumafuchi Shrine] - Japan Ise Jingu Devotees Association Foundation {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ōmizu Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|大水神社|version=95619690|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Water deities in Shinto]] [[Category:Shrines in Ise]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Ise City]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[Category:Setsumatsusha]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:大水神社]] [[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[ja:Ōmizu Shrine]]

No {{ill|saisenbako|ja|賽銭箱|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (offering box) is present. The shrine grounds cover 228 m² (approximately 69 tsubo).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 244 According to the ''Enryaku Gishikichō,'' the ancient precinct covered about 24,793 m² but has been reduced due to encroachments from private houses, temples, and the Hayasakibunko. Parts of the grounds on the north and east sides are lined with {{ill|stone walls|ja|石垣|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Ōmizu Shrine == Ōmizu Shrine is Ranked 16th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–10 The enshrined deity is Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto (大山祇御祖命), sometimes written as "大山'''罪乃'''御祖命" in old texts.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 10Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 243 This deity is considered a child of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊弉冉命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, worshipped as a mountain deity. Despite its name, meaning "great water shrine," the enshrined deity is a mountain god, which has puzzled some scholars. == Kawaai Shrine == Kawaai Shrine is Ranked 10th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 13–14 The enshrined deity is Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami (細川水神), literally "narrow river water deity," a river god. == Kumabuchi Shrine == Kumabuchi Shrine is Ranked 11th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine. The enshrined deity is Takiootogi-no-Kami (多支大刀自神), a guardian deity of sacred springs, considered a child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命). == History == According to the ''Jingū Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines Register'' issued in 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine was founded during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' records that a {{ill|shukube|ja|祝部|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was appointed to the shrine, with reconstruction handled by local officials. A record from 1482 confirms that the shrine still existed at that time. After falling into disrepair in the medieval period, the shrine was revived in 1664 under the leadership of {{ill|Kawabe no Masanaga|ja|河邊精長|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In 1871, Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine were moved to share the same grounds as Ōmizu Shrine. These two shrines were originally located upstream along the {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Rituals == The rituals at Ōmizu Shrine follow the same schedule as those at {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Ceremonies are held during the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (June & December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (October), and {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (November).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 250 Other rituals, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (January 3), National Foundation Day Festival (February 11), {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (February 23), and {{ill|Kaze-no-hi-no-matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (May & August), are conducted remotely. == Flora == A 1977 survey of the shrine grounds identified 30 trees, including camphor, cedar, fig, chinquapin, and Japanese yew. == Access == ; To Uji Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|qq=draft}} bus from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (JR) or {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|Ujiyamada Station|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Get off at the "Naikū-mae" stop.Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Grand Shrine (Inner Shrine) Information]" (Accessed October 13, 2013) * Accessible from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} via Mie Kotsu bus. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or {{ill|National Route 23|ja|国道23号|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from {{ill|Ise IC|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Parking is available nearby. ; From Uji Bridge * About 5 minutes (240 m) on foot from Uji Bridge rotary and Naikū-mae parking lot. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise Jingu|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Ise Bunka-sha (ed.) ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Pilgrimage'' (Supplement to ''Isejin''), Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (ed.) ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1'', Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * {{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ''Let's Go to Ise Jingu'', Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * {{ill|Shikinaisha Research Association|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft研究会|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (ed.) ''Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 6 Tōkaidō 1'', Kōgakkan University Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pages. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m01/0103.html Ōmizu Shrine (大水神社) - Co-located with Kawai Shrine and Kumafuchi Shrine] - Japan Ise Jingu Devotees Association Foundation {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{translated page|ja|大水神社|version=95619690|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Water deities in Shinto]] [[Category:Shrines in Ise]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Ise City]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[Category:Setsumatsusha]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:大水神社]] [[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[ja:Ōmizu Shrine]]

{{infobox Shinto shrine |name= Ōmizu Shrine, Kawaai Shrine, Kumabuchi Shrine |image= [[File:Ōmizu-jinja 01.jpg|270px]] |location= {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Uji Imazai-kechō, Hayasakiguchi 163-1, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} |latitude_deg = 34 |latitude_min = 27 |latitude_sec = 33.8 |longitude_deg = 136 |longitude_min = 43 |longitude_sec = 17.5 |map= Japan Mie |enshrined_deity= Ōmizu Shrine: Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto
Kawaai Shrine: Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami
Kumabuchi Shrine: Takiootogi-no-Kami |status= Ōmizu Shrine: {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (minor), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} auxiliary shrine
Kawaai Shrine & Kumabuchi Shrine: Kōtai Jingū subordinate shrines |established= Traditionally attributed to the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} |main_hall_style= [[Shinmei-zukuri]] |alternate_names= |sacred_sites= |annual_festival= |rituals= {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} }} '''Ōmizu Shrine''' (大水神社) is an auxiliary shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|zh|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This article also covers the subordinate shrines '''Kawaai Shrine''' (川相神社) and '''Kumabuchi Shrine''' (熊淵神社), which are co-located with Ōmizu Shrine. All three shrines are said to have been established by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Ōmizu Shrine enshrines a {{ill|Mountain God|ja|山の神|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, while Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine enshrine {{ill|water deities|ja|水神|1=Suijin|lt=water god|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 == Overview == These three shrines are located at Uji Imazai-kechō, Hayasakiguchi 163-1, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, about 200 meters from the {{ill|Uji Bridge (Ise)|ja|宇治橋|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at the entrance to the Inner Shrine's sacred precinct.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 111 About 50 meters ahead is {{ill|Aedo Hashihime Shrine|ja|饗土橋姫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and 70 meters ahead are the auxiliary shrines {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shinkawa Shrine|ja|新川神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Ishii Shrine|ja|石井神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, making a cluster of seven shrines.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): pp. 111, 116 The former {{ill|Hayasakibunko|ja|林崎文庫|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, predecessor of the {{ill|Jingū Library|ja|神宮文庫|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, is also located here.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 117 After ascending 12 stone steps, there is a {{ill|shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and a single shrine building where all three shrines are co-located. An ancient camphor tree with a trunk diameter over 2 meters covers the shrine.Gakken Publishing (2013): pp. 54–55 The shrine, built in [[shinmei-zukuri]] style with plank roofing, faces south and is surrounded by a single {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (sacred fence).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 245 The shrine measures approximately 1.8 m wide, 1.2 m deep, and 2.4 m high. No {{ill|saisenbako|ja|賽銭箱|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (offering box) is present. The shrine grounds cover 228 m² (approximately 69 tsubo).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 244 According to the ''Enryaku Gishikichō,'' the ancient precinct covered about 24,793 m² but has been reduced due to encroachments from private houses, temples, and the Hayasakibunko. Parts of the grounds on the north and east sides are lined with {{ill|stone walls|ja|石垣|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Ōmizu Shrine == Ōmizu Shrine is Ranked 16th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–10 The enshrined deity is Ōyamazumi-no-Mioya-no-Mikoto (大山祇御祖命), sometimes written as "大山'''罪乃'''御祖命" in old texts.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 10Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 243 This deity is considered a child of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊弉冉命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, worshipped as a mountain deity. Despite its name, meaning "great water shrine," the enshrined deity is a mountain god, which has puzzled some scholars. == Kawaai Shrine == Kawaai Shrine is Ranked 10th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 13–14 The enshrined deity is Hosokawa Mizu-no-Kami (細川水神), literally "narrow river water deity," a river god. == Kumabuchi Shrine == Kumabuchi Shrine is Ranked 11th among the 16 subordinate shrines of the Inner Shrine. The enshrined deity is Takiootogi-no-Kami (多支大刀自神), a guardian deity of sacred springs, considered a child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命). == History == According to the ''Jingū Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines Register'' issued in 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine was founded during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' records that a {{ill|shukube|ja|祝部|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was appointed to the shrine, with reconstruction handled by local officials. A record from 1482 confirms that the shrine still existed at that time. After falling into disrepair in the medieval period, the shrine was revived in 1664 under the leadership of {{ill|Kawabe no Masanaga|ja|河邊精長|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In 1871, Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine were moved to share the same grounds as Ōmizu Shrine. These two shrines were originally located upstream along the {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Rituals == The rituals at Ōmizu Shrine follow the same schedule as those at {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Ceremonies are held during the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (June & December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (October), and {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (November).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 250 Other rituals, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (January 3), National Foundation Day Festival (February 11), {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (February 23), and {{ill|Kaze-no-hi-no-matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (May & August), are conducted remotely. == Flora == A 1977 survey of the shrine grounds identified 30 trees, including camphor, cedar, fig, chinquapin, and Japanese yew. == Access == ; To Uji Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|qq=draft}} bus from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (JR) or {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|Ujiyamada Station|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Get off at the "Naikū-mae" stop.Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Grand Shrine (Inner Shrine) Information]" (Accessed October 13, 2013) * Accessible from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} via Mie Kotsu bus. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or {{ill|National Route 23|ja|国道23号|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from {{ill|Ise IC|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Parking is available nearby. ; From Uji Bridge * About 5 minutes (240 m) on foot from Uji Bridge rotary and Naikū-mae parking lot. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise Jingu|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Ise Bunka-sha (ed.) ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Pilgrimage'' (Supplement to ''Isejin''), Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (ed.) ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1'', Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * {{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ''Let's Go to Ise Jingu'', Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * {{ill|Shikinaisha Research Association|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft研究会|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (ed.) ''Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 6 Tōkaidō 1'', Kōgakkan University Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pages. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m01/0103.html Ōmizu Shrine (大水神社) - Co-located with Kawai Shrine and Kumafuchi Shrine] - Japan Ise Jingu Devotees Association Foundation {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{translated page|ja|大水神社|version=95619690|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Water deities in Shinto]] [[Category:Shrines in Ise]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Ise City]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[Category:Setsumatsusha]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:大水神社]] [[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[ja:Ōmizu Shrine]]

After falling into disrepair in the medieval period, the shrine was revived in 1664 under the leadership of {{ill|Kawabe no Masanaga|ja|河邊精長|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In 1871, Kawaai Shrine and Kumabuchi Shrine were moved to share the same grounds as Ōmizu Shrine. These two shrines were originally located upstream along the {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Rituals == The rituals at Ōmizu Shrine follow the same schedule as those at {{ill|Tsunaga Shrine|ja|津長神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Ceremonies are held during the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (June & December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (October), and {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (November).Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 250 Other rituals, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (January 3), National Foundation Day Festival (February 11), {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (February 23), and {{ill|Kaze-no-hi-no-matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (May & August), are conducted remotely. == Flora == A 1977 survey of the shrine grounds identified 30 trees, including camphor, cedar, fig, chinquapin, and Japanese yew. == Access == ; To Uji Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|qq=draft}} bus from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (JR) or {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|Ujiyamada Station|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Get off at the "Naikū-mae" stop.Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Grand Shrine (Inner Shrine) Information]" (Accessed October 13, 2013) * Accessible from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} via Mie Kotsu bus. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or {{ill|National Route 23|ja|国道23号|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from {{ill|Ise IC|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Parking is available nearby. ; From Uji Bridge * About 5 minutes (240 m) on foot from Uji Bridge rotary and Naikū-mae parking lot. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise Jingu|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Ise Bunka-sha (ed.) ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Pilgrimage'' (Supplement to ''Isejin''), Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (ed.) ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1'', Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * {{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ''Let's Go to Ise Jingu'', Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * {{ill|Shikinaisha Research Association|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft研究会|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (ed.) ''Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 6 Tōkaidō 1'', Kōgakkan University Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pages. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m01/0103.html Ōmizu Shrine (大水神社) - Co-located with Kawai Shrine and Kumafuchi Shrine] - Japan Ise Jingu Devotees Association Foundation {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{translated page|ja|大水神社|version=95619690|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Water deities in Shinto]] [[Category:Shrines in Ise]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Ise City]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[Category:Setsumatsusha]] }} [[ja:大水神社]] [[sn:Ōmizu Shrine]] [[ja:Ōmizu Shrine]]
